segments.s177-g41: یکی introduces a single item ('one / a'); the noun phrase تاج با یاره و گوشوار reads 'a crown with yāre and earrings', where با is the preposition 'with' linking the crown to its accompanying things and و simply joins the two items. The sequence keeps a compact poetic word order: determiner + head noun + prepositional accompaniment.
segments.s177-g42: یکی again introduces the next item ('another/one'), طوق is the head noun 'necklace / collar'; پر before گوهر means 'full (of)', so پر گوهر = 'filled with jewels', and شاهوار is an adjective meaning 'kingly / regal' that follows the noun phrase as a descriptive epithet.
segments.s177-g43: به زر و به گوهر uses the preposition به twice to mark accompaniment/material ('with / in gold and with / in gems'); بیاراست is a past tense verb meaning 'adorned / arranged'; گاه at the end is an adverb 'at times' or 'occasionally' qualifying the action. Word order (prepositional phrases before verb) is typical in classical Persian.
segments.s177-g44: چنان چون is a doubled demonstrative idiom meaning 'such as / in such a way that'; بباید is a modal/present-tense-form meaning 'ought / should / it is fitting' (classical usage), and سزاوار شاه is a predicate meaning 'fit/appropriate for the king' (sزاوار before the possessor). The clause states that the manner of adorning is what properly suits a king.
segments.s177-g45: سراسر همه شهر is an emphatic adverbial phrase 'throughout the whole city' (سراسر + همه = entire extent), آیین is the object 'the rite/ceremony', and ببست (past) here means 'he arranged/established/held' in the sense of setting up the ceremony — the verb follows the object as normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s177-g46: بیاراست is the past verb 'he adorned / decorated'; میدان و جای نشست is a coordinated noun phrase 'the square (meydān) and the place of sitting/seat', where جای specifies the spot and نشست denotes the sitting/seat (here used nominally). The verb applies to both coordinated objects.
segments.s177-g47: مهان 'guests' is the noun, modified by the adjective سرافراز 'honored / lofty' which follows it in typical Persian adjective placement; برخاستند is a past plural verb 'they rose / stood up', agreeing with مهان. The clause is a straightforward noun + adjective + verb structure.
segments.s177-g48: پذیره شدن is a deverbal noun phrase 'the receiving / the reception' (شدن forming the verbal noun/gerund); را is the definite object marker marking that phrase as the object, and بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned / prepared', so the whole clause means 'they prepared the reception.'
segments.s177-g225: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; بشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (the verb follows چو). زین‌گونه گفتار is the object phrase 'such speech' or 'words thus spoken' with زین‌گونه (thus/this kind) modifying گفتار (speech); شاه is the agent/possessor 'the king' (ʻthe king's speech') and here indicates whose speech it was.
segments.s177-g226: بفرمود is the past honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that' and is followed by بازگردد, a third-person singular present-subjunctive form used after تا to express the commanded action 'return'. به راه is the prepositional phrase 'to the road/path' marking direction or destination.
segments.s177-g227: بر here functions as a prepositional/addresses particle 'to/unto' (classical usage before names). طوس names the hero Tūs (not the city) and و links to گودرز (Goudarz). کشوادگان is an archaic/epic epithet applied to them (textual/variant reading); its precise meaning is uncertain but it functions as a descriptive title.
segments.s177-g228: گزیده 'chosen/selected' and سرافراز 'lofty/proud' are adjectival epithets that precede the noun آزادگان 'the free ones/freemen', forming a compact descriptive phrase (adjectives stacked before the plural noun).
segments.s177-g229: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. بر درگه آیند literally 'come to the court' — بر + درگه marks motion toward the royal court, and آیند is a present/subjunctive/relative plural 'they come' used here as a general/habitual present. بی‌انجمن is an adverbial phrase 'without company/without entourage' modifying آمدن.
segments.s177-g230: چنان چون is a correlative pair meaning 'just as/in the way that'; بباید is the present/modal 'ought/must' expressing what is proper; به نزدیک من 'toward/into my proximity' means 'in my presence/near me' with من here functioning as the object (me). The whole phrase qualifies how they should come.
segments.s177-g231: بشد is a narrative past 'it happened / they came' (classical epic usage) introducing the action; the verb appears before the names (verb-first order is common in older Persian narrative). طوس و گودرز are the two named subjects; نزدیک شاه indicates their motion/position 'near the king'.
segments.s177-g232: زبان برگشادند is an idiom 'they opened their tongues' = 'they began to speak' (زبان = speech/utterance here, برگشادند = they opened/revealed, past plural). بر پیش گاه is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the court/throne' with بر + پیش گاه marking location 'before the royal presence'.
segments.s177-g65: بزرگان ایران همه پیش اوی is a noun phrase + locative: بزرگان ایران ‘the great men of Iran’ (noun + genitive), همه ‘all/everyone’ quantifies them, and پیش اوی ‘before him’ is a prepositional phrase where پیش = “in the presence of” and اوی is an older/poetic third‑person pronoun ‘him’. Word order places the quantifier before the locative.
segments.s177-g66: یکایک (one by one) is an adverb modifying the past verb نهادند (3rd person plural past of نهاد#نه, here ‘placed/set’). بر خاک is the prepositional phrase ‘on the ground’, and روی (literally ‘face’) functions as the affected part — i.e. ‘placing (their) faces upon the earth’; the possessed pronoun is implied.
segments.s177-g67: وزان جایگه links temporally/locatively to the previous action: وزان = ‘from that’ (from that time/place) and جایگه = ‘place/position’. شاد گشتند is a predicate where شاد (happy) appears before the past verb گشتند (3rd pl. of گشت#گرد ‘become’), and باز (again) is an adverb emphasizing recurrence.
segments.s177-g68: فروزنده شد is a predicative sequence: فروزنده ‘radiant/illuminating’ + شد (become) = ‘became radiant’. بخت is the grammatical subject ‘fortune’, and گردن‌فراز is an epithet modifying بخت (a postpositive adjective/epithet meaning ‘proud’ or ‘pride‑raising’). The order (adjective + شد) is typical poetic inversion.
segments.s177-g69: ببوسید is a past tense verb ‘he kissed’ (the prefix ب- here appears in an older/poetic form), taking چشم و سر ‘eyes and head’ as its objects. گیو is the named subject tied to the next verb, and گفت is the reporting verb ‘said’ introducing the following clause (a quoted or content clause).
segments.s177-g70: که is the complementizer ‘that/which’ introducing the reported clause. بیرون کشیدی is a verb phrase with کشیدی (2nd person singular past ‘you pulled/drew’) and بیرون ‘out’ as adverbial, سپهر is the direct object ‘the sky/heavens’, and از نهفت ‘from concealment’ (از + نهفت) gives the source phrase — i.e. ‘you drew the sky out from hiding.’
segments.s177-g71: گزارنده is an agent noun ‘bringer/messenger’. It governs the following nouns خواب و جنگی (‘sleep and war’), where جنگی is the adjectival/relational form related to جنگ. توی here is the locative/locutional particle ‘in/within’ placed at the end to indicate the bringer operates “in/within you” (poetic order).
segments.s177-g72: گه introduces a temporal/occasional sense ‘when/at the time’. چاره مرد = ‘a man’s remedy’ (چاره ‘remedy/solution’ + مرد ‘man’), and درنگی توی is a noun phrase ‘a delay/pause in you’ (درنگی = ‘a pause/delay’, توی = ‘in/within’). Together: ‘when the remedy for a man is needed, there is a delay in you.’
segments.s177-g81: بر آیین جهانی is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase placed before the verb: بر (preposition) + آیین (custom/ceremony) + جهانی (qualifying adjective). شد (past of شدن) functions here as a copular/auxiliary 'became/was' and links to the predicate آراسته (adorned). The sequence is poetic: the circumstantial phrase comes first, then the copula + adjectival predicate.
segments.s177-g82: در و بام و دیوار is a simple coordinate list (doors, roof, and walls). The final word پرخواسته is an adjectival predicate modifying that entire list: پر (here 'full') + خواسته ('desired') = 'full of desirable things'. In poetic Persian the adjective follows the nouns it qualifies.
segments.s177-g83: نشسته is a past participle/adjectival verb meaning 'seated' or 'placed'. It is followed by the locative phrase به هر جای ('in/at every place'), and رامشگران is the plural noun 'attendants/comforters' that is understood as the ones seated. Word order is participle + locative + actor, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s177-g84: This is a nominal series of fragrant/food items: گلاب (rosewater) و (and) می‌و (an archaic form referring to wine/juices) مشک (musk) با زعفران (with saffron). با introduces an instrument/attribute ('with saffron'). The list stands as the accompaniment or contents associated with the attendants.
segments.s177-g85: همه یال اسپان is a compressed nominative phrase meaning 'the manes of all the horses' (همه = all; یال = mane; اسپان = horses). پر از is the adjectival construction 'full of' followed by the substances مشک و می (musk and wine). The verb is implied/predicative: 'were full of'.
segments.s177-g86: درم با شکر ریخته is a short clause: درم (dirhams/coins) با شکر (with sugar) ریخته (scattered/poured) — a past participle used predicatively. زیر پی is a locative phrase 'under the feet' (زیر = under; پی = foot/track), i.e. strewn beneath their hooves. The overall meaning: coins and sugar were strewn underfoot.
segments.s177-g87: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. کاووس کی is the name with the archaic title کی (Kay/Ki) appended — a formulaic epithet in Shahnameh — and روی خسرو is the direct object 'the face of Khosrow' (روی = face; خسرو = Khosrow). بدید is the simple past 'saw'. The structure is: When Kavus (the Kay) saw Khosrow's face...
segments.s177-g88: سرشکش is a diminutive noun 'a little tear / teardrop'. ز مژگان ('from the eyelashes') gives the source. به رخ بر is a stacked locative/directional sequence ('onto the cheek/face') and چکید is the past verb 'dropped/fell'. The clause literal order is source → path → goal → verb: 'a teardrop from the lashes fell onto the cheek.'
segments.s177-g89: فرود آمد is a classical compound past (فرود + آمد) meaning “he descended/stepped down.” از تخت is an ablative phrase “from the throne.” و connects two coordinated actions. شد پیش اوی uses شد (here a past auxiliary/verb meaning roughly ‘became’ → ‘approached’) plus the archaic enclitic pronoun اوی (literary form of او) meaning “him,” so the whole phrase means “and he came/stood before him.” The sequence shows typical poetic inversion: verb phrases before the pronoun-object.
segments.s177-g90: بمالید is a simple past (3rd person) of مالیدن, “he rubbed” or “he pressed/laid against.” بر is the preposition “on/upon.” چشم او is literally “his eye” (چشم + enclitic pronoun او = his/ him) and functions as an object of بمالید; چشم و روی lists two objects (“eye and face”). Word order is object(s) after the verb, with the possessive pronoun attached to the first noun.
segments.s177-g91: جوان جهانجوی is a noun phrase in apposition or with an adjectival compound: جوان (young/young man) qualified by جهانجوی (world-seeking/ambitious). بردش is a verb plus enclitic object ش (him); here it functions as a transitive past verb “took/led him” (the provided lemma senses do not include the verbal sense, so the match is irregular). نماز is the direct or directional object meaning “prayer” (i.e. led him to prayer or made him pray).
segments.s177-g92: گرازان is the plural subject (literally ‘a troop’ or ‘company’ in the provided senses) and is followed by the directional سوی + object تخت “toward the throne.” رفتند is the past plural verb “they went” and باز is an adverb “back/again,” placed after the verb to mark return. The clause therefore reads ‘the retinue went back toward the throne.’
segments.s177-g93: فراوان is an adverb placed up front meaning “frequently/many (times)/plentifully.” ز ترکان is the ablative/genitive ‘from the Turks.’ بپرسید is the past (3rd person) of پرسیدن with the literary prefix بـ, “he asked/queried.” شاه at the end is the subject in typical Persian/poetic order where the verb often appears before its nominal subject.
segments.s177-g94: هم is an additive particle “also/too.” از تخت is “from (or at) the throne.” سالار توران سپاه is a noun phrase with سالار (commander/chief) modified by توران (of Turan) and supplemented by سپاه (army/host) — i.e. “the commander of Turan’s army.” This short clause lacks an explicit verb because it continues or specifies what was asked; such nominal phrases commonly follow in poetic prose.
segments.s177-g95: چنین پاسخ آورد is a fixed poetic sequence ‘thus he brought/gave an answer’ (آورد here = ‘gave/said’). کان is an archaic particle introducing a clause meaning roughly “that” (literary/relative). کم خرد is a nominal phrase meaning “little‑witted / small‑minded” (کم modifying خرد “wisdom/mind”); it is the predicate of the introduced clause.
segments.s177-g96: به بد روی is a prepositional phrase literally ‘to/for an ill‑faced one’ (به + adjective-noun). گیتی is the direct object ‘the world.’ همی بسپرد is a literary verbal form: همی + بسپرد (بسپرد, present/subjunctive of سپردن) gives a classical sense of ‘would entrust/shall consign.’ Together the clause means ‘would entrust the world to an ill‑faced (person).’
segments.s177-g217: بدیشان is the fused prepositional phrase به ایشان ('to them' / 'for them'). رسد is a third‑person singular verb used optatively/implicative here ('may it reach / befall'). تخت شاهنشهی is the noun phrase 'the imperial throne' (تخت = 'throne' as subject; شاهنشهی is an adjectival/construct element ‘imperial’ modifying تخت). Word order is verb phrase + indirect object + subject for poetic effect.
segments.s177-g218: سرآید is a verb meaning 'to come/arrive' used here like a jussive/optative ('may ... come'). به ما is the dative 'to us'. روزگار مهی is a noun phrase: روزگار 'time/season' modified by مهی 'beautiful, moon‑like' — 'a beautiful time/era'. The clause expresses a wished/anticipated state for 'us'.
segments.s177-g219: This is a nominal phrase: خردمند مردی 'a wise man' (خردمند qualifying مردی), joined by و to جوینده راه 'a seeker of the way' (جوینده governing راه). In Persian poetry adjectives or titles can precede the noun; the two coordinated noun phrases form the direct object of 'sent'.
segments.s177-g220: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent' (3rd person). نزدیک here introduces the destination 'to the presence of / near' and governs the following name: نزدیک کاووس شاه = 'to King Kavus' (poetic expression for 'to the king'). The proper name کاووس is followed by the title شاه.
segments.s177-g221: که begins the subordinate/conditional clause ('that' / introducing what follows); از ما یکی‌گر is a compressed conditional phrase meaning 'if one of us' (از = 'of/from', ما = 'us', یکی‌گر = 'if one' or 'should one'); برین دشت is 'on/in this plain' where بر + ین is the older poetic form of برِ این ('on this') and دشت is the noun 'plain' or 'wilderness'. The segment lacks the main verb (placed in the next segment), so it functions as the protasis ('if ...') of the conditional.
segments.s177-g222: نهد is the 3rd person singular verb 'to place/set' used here in the classical present/subjunctive sense completing the conditional ('he would place' / 'should he place'); بر کمان = 'upon the bow' (بر = 'on', کمان = 'bow'); پر تیر is a poetic, ezāfe-less phrase 'full of arrows' (پر = 'full (of)', تیر = 'arrow'); خدنگ ('dagger') follows as a further object or appositive—poetic compression omits linking particles, so these nouns are the things placed on/with the bow.
segments.s177-g223: یکی here = 'someone' or 'a certain person'; کینه خیزد is a light verb construction meaning 'a hatred rises' or 'someone kindles/harbors a grudge' (کینه = 'hatred/grudge', خیزد = 'arises/comes up' — classical present/impersonal use); که introduces the following clause ('and/that'), and افراسیاب is the proper-name subject of that clause. Grammatically this segment states that a person will take up hostility, leading into what Afrasiab will see.
segments.s177-g224: هم امشب = 'also/too tonight' (هم = 'also', امشب = 'tonight'); همی before the verb is the archaic auxiliary/aspect marker used in epic Persian to indicate ongoing or imminent action (here functioning like a future/imperf. marker: 'will/shall'); آن is the demonstrative 'that' (referring to the thing just mentioned), ببیند is 3rd person singular present/subjunctive/imperf. 'to see' (used here as 'will see'), and به خواب means 'in a dream' (به = 'in/into/with', خواب = 'dream'). Together: 'Afrasiab will likewise see that tonight in a dream.'
segments.s177-g57: ستودش is the past tense of ستودن with the object suffix -ش ‘him’ (’praised him’). فراوان is an adverb modifying the verb (’greatly’). و coordinates the clauses, and کرد آفرین is an idiomatic compound: کرد (to do/make) plus the noun آفرین (an acclamation), meaning ‘made an acclamation’ / ‘cried bravo’.
segments.s177-g58: چنین (’thus/so’) modifies گفت (’said’); این جمله introduces direct speech. کای is the vocative particle ‘O’ addressing the addressee, followed by شهریار زمین ‘king/sovereign of the earth’. The verb گفت is the past-tense reporting verb (‘said’).
segments.s177-g59: ز تو is the prepositional phrase ‘from/away from you’ (poetic ز = به/از). چشم بدخواه is a noun phrase ‘the ill-wisher’s eye’ (چشم + بدخواه). The second تو is the remoteness/beneficiary argument of دور (’far from you’). باد is the optative particle expressing a wish (‘may it be’).
segments.s177-g60: روان سیاوش is a genitive/possessive sequence ‘Siavash’s soul/spirit’. پر از نور is a predicate meaning ‘full of light’ (پر = full (of), از = of). باد again is the optative particle ‘may it be’, forming a parallel wish to the previous clause.
segments.s177-g61: جهاندار یزدان is a nominative apposition ‘the world-ruler, God’. گوای functions as ‘witness’ (the thing doing the witnessing). منست is an archaic fused form (من + است) meaning roughly ‘is mine / is my’ in this construction, so گوای منست = ‘is my witness’.
segments.s177-g62: که is the subordinating conjunction ‘that’ introducing the content clause. دیدار تو ('your sight' / 'seeing you') is the subject of the subordinate clause, and رهنمای منست is the predicate ‘is my guide’ (رهنمای = guide; منست = archaic copula/possessive as above).
segments.s177-g63: سیاووش را uses the object marker را to mark the definite/ topicalized noun ‘Siavash’. زنده‌گر is an epithet/appositive meaning ‘reviver’ or ‘life‑giver’ applied to Siavash. دیدمی is the archaic first‑person past form (‘I saw’), common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s177-g64: بدین گونه (به + این گونه) = ‘in this manner / thus’ (here tokenized into parts: ب = the prepositional prefix ‘به’, دین is the remaining element of the contraction, and گونه = ‘manner’). از دل = ‘from the heart’ (i.e. genuinely). نخندیدمی is the negative past first‑person (‘I did not laugh’), with the archaic negation prefix ن- and past form -یدمی.
segments.s177-g73: سوی خانه پهلوان is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward/to the house of the champion' placed before the verb (classical Persian allows the direction before the verb). آمدند is past tense, third‑person plural 'they came/arrived' and agrees with the omitted subject (they).
segments.s177-g74: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; شاد و روشن‌روان is a coordinate adjective phrase ('happy and bright‑souled/cheerful') modifying that subject. The verb آمدند repeats for rhythmic/paratactic effect and remains past third‑person plural.
segments.s177-g75: ببودند is an archaic/past plural form meaning 'they were/remained'. یک هفته is a duration phrase 'for a week' modifying the verb. با می‌بدست is an instrumental/adjunct phrase 'with wine in hand' (می = wine, بدست = in hand/by hand), placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s177-g76: بیاراسته precedes and qualifies بزمگاه; in the surface text it reads as 'unadorned' or 'not ornamented' and functions adjectivally. The conjunction و links that noun phrase to نشست, where نشست is past third‑person singular/plural 'they sat' (here plural contextually) meaning 'took their seats'.
segments.s177-g77: به هشتم is a temporal phrase 'on the eighth [day]' (به used as a temporal marker). سوی شهر کاووس شاه is a directional phrase 'toward the city of Kavus the king' where the title/name follows the noun (city of Kavus, the king).
segments.s177-g78: همه شاددل is the subject phrase 'all, cheerful‑hearted'. برگرفتند is a compound past verb 'they took up / lifted' used idiomatically with راه ('road') to mean 'they set out' or 'they took the road' (past, third‑person plural).
segments.s177-g79: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. کیخسرو is the subject (proper name) and آمد is the past tense 'came'. بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/ before the sovereign/king' with بر marking direction/tendency toward the person.
segments.s177-g80: جهان گشت is 'the world became' (گشت = became/turned). پر is a predicative adjective 'full (of)' followed by a coordinated list of nouns بوی و رنگ و نگار 'scent and color and ornament/beauty' describing what the world became full of.
segments.s177-g345: بگشتند is a past (perfective) 3rd‑person plural verb (ب‑ preverb + گشتند) meaning ‘they turned/roamed/returned’. یک هفته is a duration expression ‘for a week’ that follows the verb; گرد اندرش is a locative phrase meaning ‘around it’ (گرد = ‘around/about’, اندرش = اندر + ش = ‘in/within it’ with the enclitic ‑ش ‘its’). Together: ‘they roamed around it for a week.’
segments.s177-g346: ندیدند is the negated past tense ‘they did not see’. بدیده here functions adverbially (classical/poetic use = ‘with the eye’ / ‘to their sight’), placed before the verb for emphasis. جای درش = جای + در + ‑ش: جای ‘place’, در ‘door/gate’ (not the preposition), and ‑ش the possessive suffix ‘its’, so ‘the place of its door’ or ‘where its doorway was’. The whole segment means ‘they did not (with their eyes) see the place of its door / could not find its doorway.’
segments.s177-g347: به نومیدی (به + نومیدی) is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning ‘in/into despair’ or ‘out of despair’. از جنگ is an ablative/genitive phrase ‘from the battle’. گشتند باز is the past (perfective) 3rd‑person plural ‘they turned/returned’ with باز functioning adverbially as ‘back/again’ (word order here places the verb before the adverb, a common poetic variant). Together: ‘in despair of the battle they turned back.’
segments.s177-g348: نیامد is the negated past of آمدن ‘did not come / did not manage to come’. بر here links the verb to the following reason phrase (classical usage: نیامد بر + از ... = ‘(a return/relief) did not come because of…’ or ‘they could not return on account of…’). از رنج راه دراز is an ablative phrase ‘from the hardship/suffering of the long road’ (رنج = hardship/suffering, راه دراز = long road). Together: ‘(they) did not come/return because of the hardship of the long road.’
segments.s177-g337: This is a small clause meaning “the spahbed said to Fereyberz.” Subject first (سپهبد), then the addressee marked by the object/topic marker را (فریبرز را), then the verb گفت. The final word مرد is not the subject here but the beginning of the reported clause (vocative/subject of the following clause).
segments.s177-g338: This is the reported clause introduced immediately after گفت: به چیزی چو آید به دشت نبرد = “when/if he comes into the plain for something.” چو + آید is a temporal/conditional ‘when/if he comes’; به چیزی ‘for something’ or ‘on some errand’; به دشت ‘into/on the plain’. The final نبرد is a nominal/verb form meaning ‘(no) fighting/battle’ here — the clause states that there is no (open) battle when a man comes into the plain for some purpose.
segments.s177-g339: A string of prepositional phrases listing arms: به گرز گران ‘with a heavy mace’; و به تیغ ‘and with a sword’; و کمند ‘and (with) a lasso/snare’. The repeated به marks instruments/means; گران is an adjective postposed to گرز (heavy mace).
segments.s177-g340: بکوشد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form meaning ‘he strives/tries.’ که introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that’; آرد is the present/subjunctive ‘he brings’ and takes the object phrase به چیزی گزند ‘harm to something’ (آوردن + گزند = to bring harm). The whole clause expresses effort to avoid bringing harm.
segments.s177-g341: به پیرامن دژ ‘around the fortress’ is a locative phrase (پیرامن = around, surrounding). یکی راه نیست literally ‘one road there is not’ — Persian permits the numeral/determiner (یکی) before the noun (راه) with the negation نیست after; together they mean ‘there is not a single path.’
segments.s177-g342: This line uses poetic order: ز آتش ‘from/of fire’ (or ‘ aflame’), کسی را دل literally ‘someone’s heart’ with را marking the affected noun, then the vocative ای شاه ‘O king’, and final نیست ‘is not’. The intended sense is negative: ‘O king, there is not anyone whose heart is not aflame’ (i.e., no one whose heart is untouched by fire).
segments.s177-g343: میان ‘in the middle/among’ + زیر جوشن ‘under the cuirass/armor’ is a locative phrase; بسوزد is an intransitive present/subjunctive ‘he burns / he is burning’ and همی is an emphatic/progressive particle meaning ‘continually’ or ‘indeed.’ Together: ‘(he) burns beneath the armor.’
segments.s177-g344: تن بارکش is a compound phrase ‘the load‑bearing body’ or ‘the burdened body’ (تن = body, بارکش = bearing a load). برفروزد is a verb meaning ‘he kindles/sets alight/illuminates’ and همی again marks continuous or ongoing action. The clause says the body is continually kindled/sorely affected.
segments.s177-g9: گزین کرد is a compound past: گزین (choose/select) + کرد (past auxiliary 'did/made') yields 'he chose.' ازان نامداران is the object: ازان ('of those' / 'from those') + نامداران ('the famed/renowned ones'). زم here is a short temporal connector meaning 'then/when' (classical/poetic use).
segments.s177-g10: بگفت is a speech verb 'he said/told.' آنچ is a relative/indefinite pronoun 'that which/what.' بشنید is past 'he heard' and here forms the content of what was reported: 'that which he had heard.' از بیش و کم is an adverbial phrase 'with regard to more and less' i.e. 'in greater or lesser detail' (literal 'from more and less').
segments.s177-g11: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is the prepositional/clitic form 'to him,' followed by گفت (speech verb) used as 'said.' ز ایدر برو باصفهان is an imperative direction: ز ایدر 'from here,' برو 'go' (imperative), باصفهان 'to/ toward Isfahan' (بـ + اسم place), so word order is 'from here go to Isfahan.'
segments.s177-g12: بر here means 'toward/to' (direction). نیو is a short place/name or regional term (contextual/archaic); گودرز is the proper name 'Goudarz.' کشوادگان is an epithet/group name (archaic/variant form). The whole phrase gives the destination/person to whom the addressee should go: 'toward Niv, to Goudarz of the Keshvadagans.'
segments.s177-g13: بگویش is the imperative/optative 'tell him' (بگو + ـیش 'to him'). که introduces a subordinate content clause 'that...' KiKhosrow آمد به زم = 'Kay Khosrow came' with به + زم giving the temporal sense 'at the time when' (زم = 'when/at the time that').
segments.s177-g14: The second 'که' again introduces explanation/detail ('that'). بادی is an adjective/noun 'windy' or 'a wind' (here 'a wind'). نجست is past 'sprang/leapt' (from جست) — 'sprang up.' از بر او is a prepositional phrase 'from before him' i.e. 'in front of him / upon him.' دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' qualifying either the wind or the scene: 'a sullen wind sprang up before him.'
segments.s177-g15: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (used to introduce an item). نامه is 'letter.' نزدیک کاووس شاه is a genitival/locative phrase 'near/for King Kavus' — here functioning as the recipient/target of the letter ('a letter for King Kavus' / 'a letter addressed to King Kavus').
segments.s177-g16: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite agent noun 'a (one) sent' / 'a messenger' (the -ای makes it indefinite). چست is an adjective 'nimble/swift' modifying that messenger. بگرفت راه is a past verbal phrase 'took the road / set out' (بگرفت = 'took,' راه = 'way/road'), so together 'a swift messenger took the road.'
segments.s177-g145: A nominal phrase in apposition: پیمبر (a title) + سرافراز (postpositive adjective) + گیو (proper name) + دلیر (another postpositive adjective). Persian adjectives normally follow the noun they modify; here several epithets cluster after/around the name to produce the heroic listing.
segments.s177-g146: A relative clause introduced by که referring back to Giv. چنگ یلان داشت is literal 'had the claw/grip of heroes' where داشت is the simple past 'had'; و coordinates the second phrase بازوی شیر, a noun‑noun genitive ('the arm/strength of a lion'). In classical/poetic Persian the linking ezāfe is often omitted between nouns (بازویِ شیر).
segments.s177-g147: بدو گفت = 'he said to him': بدو is the preposed indirect object ('to him'), followed by گفت (past of گفتن). The following clause با طوس نوذر بگوی is reported/indirect command: با طوس = 'by/with Tous' (i.e. using or accompanied by Tous), نوذر = the addressee/name, and بگوی is the present-subjunctive/optative form used here for reported instruction ('that he should tell' / 'tell ...').
segments.s177-g148: که introduces the subordinate content clause ('that ...'). هنگام شادی is a temporal phrase 'at the time of joy/celebration.' بهانه مجوی is an admonition: مجوی is the negative jussive/subjunctive form 'do not seek' (classical negative command), with بهانه = 'excuse, pretext.'
segments.s177-g149: A coordinate noun phrase: بزرگان ('the great ones, nobles') and گردان ایران‌زمین ('the troop/host of the land of Iran'). گردان is a collective noun here ('troop, force') modifying ایران‌زمین as its domain.
segments.s177-g150: همه is the subject 'all/everyone.' شاه را uses را as the definite-object marker (the king being definite). خواندند is past plural 'they uttered/called/sang' (here 'they cried out'), and آفرین is the acclamation shouted. Word order is subject + object (marked) + verb + interjection.
segments.s177-g151: چرا is the question word 'why.' سر کشی here functions as a verbal-nominal compound meaning 'rebellion/defiance' (literally 'raising the head'); تو is the second-person pronoun 'you.' به فرمان دیو is a prepositional phrase 'against/to the command of the demon' (فرمان + genitive دیو). The sequence asks about the addressee's defiance of that command.
segments.s177-g152: نبینی is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'do you not see / you do not see' used rhetorically. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle (classical), often 'still/indeed.' فر گیهان is a noun phrase 'the glory/splendour of the world' (ezāfe omitted in poetic diction), and خدیو is a vocative/title ('ruler' / 'khedive') addressing the person.
segments.s177-g49: برفتند is a past-tense verb (3rd pl.) meaning 'they went/departed'. هشتاد فرسنگ is a measured distance (numeral + unit), and پیش follows the distance to indicate 'ahead / before' (i.e. in front of or in advance). Word order is verb + distance phrase; the whole chunk means they moved forward by eighty farsangs.
segments.s177-g50: پذیره here is a noun 'reception/acceptance' used with شدندش: the past plural شدند plus the enclitic -ش (him/it) expresses that 'they received him' (literally 'they made him reception / they effected his reception'). به is the preposition forming an adverbial phrase 'in/according to' (here 'in the manner of'), آیین خوی (آیین + خوی + ش) = 'their own custom/ceremony' (خوی 'habit/custom', ش = 'his/their').
segments.s177-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. چشم سپهبد is a genitive phrase 'the spahbed's eye/gaze' and برآمد is a past verb 'arose/emerged/turned up' used idiomatically for 'the gaze turned'. به شاه ('to the king') marks the target of that gaze with the preposition به.
segments.s177-g52: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' emphasizing the identity; گیو is the proper name. را is the direct‑object marker attached after the object. دید is the past verb 'he saw'. با او 'with him' marks accompaniment and به راه 'on the road' is a locative phrase ('towards/on the way'). Word order places the object with its marker before the verb, as standard in Persian.
segments.s177-g53: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. آمد is simple past 'came'. پدیدار functions as 'appearance/manifest' — here part of the predicate meaning 'came into view / appeared'. با شاه گیو lists accompaniment: 'with the king, Giv' (Giv appearing in the company of the king); order is somewhat compressed (poetic) but means 'Giv appeared with the king.'
segments.s177-g54: پیاده شد(ند) is a common older Persian construction: پیاده 'on foot' + شد(ند) 'became' → 'they dismounted / went on foot'. آن سواران = 'those riders' (آن determiner + plural سواران). نیو likely modifies the riders (regional/poetic adjective 'new' or a local epithet); here it follows the noun as an adjective. Overall: 'those riders became on-foot.'
segments.s177-g55: فرو is a directional prefix/adverb 'down(ward)'; ریخت is the verb 'poured/flowed' (here concrete for tears). از دیدگان = 'from the eyes' (از 'from' + plural دیدگان 'eyes'). آب زرد is a literal description 'yellow water' (a poetic expression for tears). The particle order puts the manner/adverb before the verb (فرو ریخت).
segments.s177-g56: ز (poetic form of از) means 'because of / from'. درد here is emotional sorrow or grief. سیاوش is the personal name. بسی modifies یاد کرد and means 'much/greatly' (here 'remembered greatly'). یاد کرد is the compound 'to remember' (یاد 'remembrance' + کردن 'to do' → 'to remember'). The clause reads 'out of sorrow for Siavash he remembered [him] greatly.'
segments.s177-g177: This clause says who endured the hardships and states the result. تو (you) is the subject; این رنجها را places the definite object (رنجها + object marker را) before the verb phrase. که بردی is a relative/restrictive clause modifying رنجها (literally “that you carried/underwent”), and برست is the final predicate meaning “was ended/was removed” (poetic past-form) referring back to those hardships.
segments.s177-g178: که introduces an explanatory clause giving the reason: خسرو is the subject and جوانست is the copular predicate (“is young”); و connects a second predicate, کندآورست, a third-person (poetic) verbal form meaning “he causes/brings about.” The whole clause explains why the hardships are ended.
segments.s177-g179: کسی introduces an indefinite/relative idea “someone/anyone”; کاو here functions as an archaic relative/connecting element ('who/that') linking to بود (the copula). بود (poetic present/past form) links the subject to the predicate شهریار زمین ("king/ruler of the land"); the order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s177-g180: هنر is the quality named as necessary and is followed by باید, an impersonal modal meaning “is necessary/one must have.” The following nouns joined by و (گوهر و فر و دین) are additional required attributes; the copula/necessity expressed by باید applies to the whole coordinated list.
segments.s177-g181: This is a name phrase in apposition: فریبرز is the personal name, کاووس names his father, and فرزند شاه (‘son of the king’) specifies the relationship. The three nouns are stacked without additional particles in typical epic/poetic appositive order.
segments.s177-g182: سزاوارتر is the comparative of سزاوار (“deserving”) meaning “more deserving.” کس functions as the comparison class (“than anyone”); به + تخت و کلاه indicates the thing he is deserving of (“to/for the throne and [the] crown”). The comparative adjective precedes the phrase it governs, a common poetic order.
segments.s177-g183: بهرسو is an adverbial phrase “on every side” or “in every direction”; ز دشمن ندارد is a negated possession construction meaning “has no enemy” (ندارد = does not have). نژاد (‘lineage/race’) is the theme/subject of this statement, placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s177-g184: همش (‘all of it’/‘all together’) introduces the enumeration. فر و برزست pairs a noun (فر, royal glory) with a verb (برزست, poetic past “rose/exalted”), so “his farr rose/exalted”; the conjunctions then add هم نام و داد (“also name and justice”), where the verb is implied to apply to these items as well.
segments.s177-g25: فرستاده شد is a passive/periphrastic past: noun + شد yields 'was sent' (third person past). نزد + name means 'in the presence of' / 'to' that person (نزد here = 'before, in the presence of'). کی here functions as a relative/connector ('who' / 'that'), linking this clause to what follows.
segments.s177-g26: ز is the poetic ablative 'from'; یال هیونان is a genitive phrase 'the mane of the beasts' (یال governed by هیونان). بپالود is past tense 3rd-person singular with the verbal prefix بَـ (poetic/colloquial form) meaning 'purified/refined'. خوی is the direct object 'disposition/character' receiving the action.
segments.s177-g27: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آمد is the past tense 'came'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near', followed by the name in apposition (کاووس شاه = 'Kavus, the king'). Word order is typical: temporal + verb + directional phrase + addressee/title.
segments.s177-g28: ز شادی is an ablative phrase 'from (out of) joy'; خروش آمد is a noun + آمد construction meaning 'a cry/uproar came' (i.e., 'a cry arose'); از بارگاه adds the source 'from the court/palace'. Note the poetic duplication of the ablative prepositions ز and از to mark cause/source.
segments.s177-g29: خبر شد is an impersonal/existential past 'it became known' or 'news spread'. به گیتی 'to the world' indicates the scope. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that ...'; فرزند شاه is the noun phrase 'the son of the king' (subject of the subordinate clause).
segments.s177-g30: جهانجوی is an epithet 'world‑seeking' placed before the proper name کیخسرو; آمد is the past 'came'. ز راه is an ablative phrase 'from/by the road' indicating the manner/route of arrival. The epithet + name form is common in epic style.
segments.s177-g31: سپهبد is the subject (title 'spahbed'). فرستاده را uses را as the definite object marker ('the messenger' as direct object). پیش خواند is a compound meaning 'called/summoned into his presence' (پیش = 'in the presence of', خواند = 'called/summoned' here).
segments.s177-g32: بران is an adverbial 'thereupon' / 'on that'; نامه is 'the letter' (object of the circumstance). گیو is the subject (proper name). گوهر فشاند is verb + object (فشاند past 'scattered/sprinkled') meaning 'scattered jewels' — note the poetic word order where the adverbial and object phrases appear around the subject and verb.
segments.s177-g265: بدو (prepositional/dative clitic) means 'to him/unto him' and combines with گفت (past verb 'said') to yield 'he said to him'. طوس‌ای is a vocative: طوس (the hero Tūs) + ای (O), followed by the epithets جهاندیده پیر (two adjectives used after the name) meaning 'worldly‑wise' and 'elder'. The order reflects poetic inversion and direct address.
segments.s177-g266: سخن گوی is an imperative/hortative phrase: سخن 'speech/word' + گوی (here 'say'/'speak') → 'speak (your) speech' or 'speak up'. لیکن is a contrastive conjunction 'but / yet'. همه modifies دلپذیر and means 'all / every (one)', so همه دلپذیر = 'all are pleasant' or 'every word is delightful'. The clause thus gives a command then comments on the pleasing nature of the speech.
segments.s177-g267: اگر introduces the condition 'if'. تیغ تو هست is a simple clause 'your sword is' (هست as copula/existential). سندان شکاف is a noun‑noun compound: سندان 'anvil' + شکاف 'splitter/crack' used adjectivally — i.e. 'anvil‑splitting' — a poetic epithet describing the sword's power.
segments.s177-g268: سنانم is سنان + ـم 'my spear' (possessive suffix). بدرد is here a verbal idea 'will pierce / will wound' (literally 'cause pain'), applied to the spear as subject. دل کوه قاف is the object phrase 'the heart of Mount Qaf' (دل = heart/center; کوه قاف = Mount Qaf). Word order is Subject Verb Object with the possessive clitic attached to the subject.
segments.s177-g269: وگر is a conditional 'and if / but if'. گرز تو هست 'your mace is' (هست as copula). با سنگ و تاب is a prepositional phrase 'with stone(s) and تاب'; here سنگ likely means precious stones/gems and تاب is used as 'brightness / radiance', so the phrase describes the mace as ornamented with gems and shining brilliance.
segments.s177-g270: خدنگم = خدنگ + ـم 'my dagger'. بدوزد is a present/subjunctive/epic imperfect of دوختن 'to sew/stitch' used metaphorically here ('will stitch / will pierce into'). دل آفتاب is the object 'the heart of the sun' (دل = heart/center; آفتاب = sun). The image is metaphorical and the verb is used vividly rather than literally.
segments.s177-g271: وگر introduces a conditional/contrast 'and if'. تو ز کشواد داری نژاد literally orders as 'you from Kashvad have lineage' (ز = 'from/of'), meaning 'if you are of Kashvad's stock'; داری is the present 'have/possess' and نژاد is the noun 'lineage/race'. The preposition ز before the name is a poetic placement marking origin.
segments.s177-g272: منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + emphatic ـم). طوس names the speaker (Tūs). نوذر مه و شاهزاد is a sequence of appositive titles/epithets; نوذر is the proper name Nowzar (here evoking a royal house), مه is used poetically (literally 'moon') as an epithet of brightness or eminence, and شاهزاد means 'prince' or 'princely'. Together they stand in apposition to identify lineage/status following the conditional clause.
segments.s177-g281: This is a pair of imperatives. بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him / unto him' placed before the verb for emphasis. تاج بسپار is the 2nd-person imperative (بسپار) meaning 'entrust (the crown)'; دل شاد دار uses دار as an archaic imperative of داشتن ('keep') so the phrase means 'keep (your) heart glad' with شاد as the adjective modifying دل.
segments.s177-g282: چو introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'if/when' here (conditional). فرزند is 'son/child' and بینی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive 'you see' (or 'you regard'); همی is an emphatic particle 'indeed/truly'; شهریار is the predicate 'king'. The clause therefore reads 'if you see/regard him as a son, he is indeed a king.'
segments.s177-g283: بدو گفت کاووس is inverted word order typical of poetry: کاووس گفت بدو = 'Kavus said to him.' گفت is the past 3rd-person of 'to say' (here 'said'). کاین رای نیست introduces the judgment: کاین = 'that this' and رای نیست = 'this counsel is not (so / is not right)'.
segments.s177-g284: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. فرزند هر دو = 'the children of both' (هر دو = 'both'); به دل is a locative phrase 'in the heart'; بر یکیست is the predicate 'are upon / amount to one' — i.e. 'are united as one in the heart.' Word order puts the locative and complement before the verbal predicate (یکیست).
segments.s177-g285: یکی را is the definite object 'one' with the object marker را. چو من کرده باشم is a subordinate conditional/comparative: چو = 'as/like' (here 'as' / 'if ... like me'), کرده باشم is a compound perfect-subjunctive/conditional ('if I have made' or 'if I have made him so'); گزین is the 2nd-person imperative 'choose.' The clause yields 'Choose one whom I have made like myself.'
segments.s177-g286: دل دیگر = 'the other's heart' (subject); از من = 'toward/from me' indicating the object of the hostility; شود is 3rd-person present/future 'becomes' or 'will become'; پر ز کین is a predicative phrase 'full of hatred' (پر = 'full (of)', ز کین = 'of hatred'). The whole means 'the other’s heart will become full of hatred toward me.'
segments.s177-g287: یکی کار سازم is first-person present/future-like 'I will make/arrange one action/plan' (سازم = 'I make / I shall make'). که here introduces the purpose/result clause. هر دو ز من = 'both (of them) toward me / from me' (i.e. 'that neither of the two ... with respect to me'), preparing the object of the following verb.
segments.s177-g288: نگیرند is negative present/subjunctive 3rd-person plural 'they do not take / may not take' (here purposive: 'so that they do not take'). کین = 'vengeance/hatred' (object of نگیرند). اندرین انجمن = 'in this assembly / in this gathering' (اندرین = 'in these', انجمن = 'assembly'). The clause thus means 'that they do not take vengeance on me in this assembly.'
segments.s177-g17: هیونان is the plural noun 'beasts/animals' (here general or monstrous creatures) followed by a string of adjectival attributes. کفک افگن is an adjectival compound: کفک ('foam') + افگن (a poetic formative meaning '‑making' or '‑spouting'), so together 'foam‑spitting' or 'foam‑flecked'. بادپای is another postpositive adjective 'wind‑footed / swift‑footed'. In classical Persian, adjectives commonly follow the noun they modify; poetically they are stacked for vivid effect.
segments.s177-g18: بجستند is simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they sprang/jumped'. برسان is the base of رساندن 'to bring' used here in a purposive/infinitival sense ('in order to bring' or 'to fetch'). آتش is the direct object 'fire'. ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from the place' (ز is the poetic form of 'from', جای = place). Together the clause means 'they leapt (so as) to bring fire from the place'; the order is slightly inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.s177-g19: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' and comes before the proper name گیو and the epithet روشن‌روان ('bright‑souled' or 'clear‑spirited') in apposition. This is a common classical style: the job/title is stated and then the name and epithet identify the person.
segments.s177-g20: نخستین here functions adverbially/adjectivally 'first' (i.e., 'first of all' or 'the first to do so'). بیامد is the past tense, 3rd‑person singular 'he came'. بر پهلوان uses بر as the preposition 'to/toward' followed by پهلوان 'the hero/champion' as the goal of his coming. The whole means 'he came first to the hero.'
segments.s177-g21: پیامش is 'his message' (پیام + enclitic possessive ش). همی گفت is a conventional classical narrative formula: همی is an archaic verbal particle giving continuous/repetitive aspect and گفت is 'said' — together functioning as 'he said' / 'he declared'. و نامه بداد is a coordinated clause 'and he gave the letter'; note that in classical orthography the past of دادن can appear as بداد (but the provided senses list does not include this verbal sense).
segments.s177-g22: This line is best read as جهان‌پهلوان (the world‑champion) as subject; نامه بر سر نهاد is a short clause meaning 'placed the letter on (his) head' where بر is the preposition 'on', سر 'head', and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place'. (In the word list 'نامه‌بر' is given as one token but here it functions as two words 'نامه بر' in the classical phrase.)
segments.s177-g23: ز بهر سیاووش is a literary prepositional phrase 'for the sake of Siyâvash' (ز بهر = 'for' / 'on behalf of'). ببارید is the simple past 3rd‑person singular of باریدن 'to fall / to rain' and here means 'water fell/poured'. آب is 'water'; the verb–object order is inverted for poetic effect (verb before its noun).
segments.s177-g24: همی کرد combines the continuous/iterative particle همی with کرد (past of کردن) to mean 'kept doing' or 'was continually doing'. نفرین is the object 'a curse'. بر is the preposition 'upon/against' and افراسیاب is the proper name 'Afrasiâb'. Thus the clause reads 'they kept pronouncing a curse against Afrasiâb.'
segments.s177-g209: همی is a classical adverb meaning ‘constantly’ or ‘always’ placed before the verb; تافت is the poetic past of تابیدن (‘to shine’), third-person singular, so together همی تافت = ‘there shone continuously’. زان تخت خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'from that throne of Khosrow' (زان = poetic 'from that') placed after the verb; چو ماه is a simile ('like the moon') modifying تافت.
segments.s177-g210: ز یاقوت here uses the poetic preposition ز = 'of/from' to mark the material/constituent ('a ruby'); رخشنده is a postpositive adjective 'radiant', and بر سر کلاه is a locative phrase 'upon the top of the hat/crown' (بر = on, سر کلاه = the crown's top). The whole phrase specifies what shone on the crown.
segments.s177-g211: غمی شد دل طوس is a verbal phrase meaning 'Tūs's heart became grief-filled' (شد = 'became'); the noun غمی precedes شد in the classical style. و اندیشه کرد coordinates a following action: اندیشه (a noun meaning 'thought') with کرد as a light verb yields 'he thought/pondered.'
segments.s177-g212: که introduces the content clause ('that/that if...'); امروز 'today' sets the time. اگر marks the conditional 'if'; من بسازم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of ساختن used for a hypothetical 'if I should cause/arrange', with بسازم as the subjunctive/potential. نبرد at the end is the object 'a battle'.
segments.s177-g213: بسی is a quantifier ('many/much' in classical usage) modifying کشته آید — کشته (the slain) with آید ('will come/occur') yields the sense 'many will be killed' or 'many slain will occur'. ز هر دو سپاه is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from each (both) of the two hosts/armies'.
segments.s177-g214: ز ایران 'from Iran' (ز = poetic 'from'); نه برخیزد is a negated verb form meaning 'shall not arise/come up' (برخیزد = 'arise/occur'); this is followed by the noun phrase این کینه‌گاه 'this seat/occasion of hatred' as the subject—classical word order allows the verb before the subject.
segments.s177-g215: نباشد is an impersonal/negative existential form meaning 'there will not be' or 'there is not'; جز = 'except'; از کام افراسیاب = 'from the desire/will of Afrasiab' (کام = desire, افراسیاب = proper name). The whole phrase means 'there will be none except from Afrasiab's desire.'
segments.s177-g216: سر بخت ترکان is a nominal phrase 'the head/onset of the Turks' fortune' (سر here = 'top/head' used figuratively, بخت = 'fate/fortune', ترکان = 'the Turks'); برآید is the third-person present/potential 'will arise/appear'; ز خواب 'from sleep' here means 'from a dream' (ز = 'from'), so the clause says the Turks' fortune will spring from a dream.
segments.s177-g185: دژم is an adjectival epithet placed before the proper name گیو to qualify his mood ('sullen/grim'). برخاست is a past-tense simple verb ('he rose/stood up') following the subject phrase; از پیش او is a prepositional phrase 'from before him / out of his presence' (از + پیش = 'from the presence of').
segments.s177-g186: که here introduces an explanatory/casual clause ('for/because'). خام آمدش is an idiomatic construction: خام ('raw/naive') + آمدش ('came to him' = 'seemed to him'), so the phrase means 'seemed immature to him.' دانش و کیش او is the noun phrase ('his learning and character/way') that is described by خام آمدش.
segments.s177-g187: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came' and is followed by the prepositional phrase به گودرز 'to Goudarz.' The sequence ends with کشواد گفت ('Keshvad said'), where گفت introduces the reported speech that follows; subjects are omitted as typical in epic narrative.
segments.s177-g188: که introduces the content of what was said ('that'). فر و خرد is a compound subject ('glory and wisdom'); نیست negates existence ('is not' / 'there is no'); با طوس جفت means 'a match/peer with Tous' (با = 'with', جفت = 'pair/match'), so the clause states that no matching glory and wisdom are found with Tous.
segments.s177-g189: دو چشمش = 'his two eyes' with the possessive clitic -ش. تو گویی is an idiomatic phrase functioning like English 'as if' or 'it seems' (literally 'one would say'); نبیند is the negative present/subjunctive form of دیدن ('not see/does not see'), and همی is a poetic habitual/continuous particle emphasizing the ongoing nature ('as if continually not seeing').
segments.s177-g190: فریبرز را uses the object marker را to mark the definite object ('Fariburz'); برگزیند is a present/habitual third-person verb 'chooses/selects' and with the particle همی indicates a repeated or continuing action ('keeps choosing').
segments.s177-g191: برآشفت is a past-tense verb meaning 'he became angry' (an intransitive change-of-state). The sequence 'گودرز و گفت' places the character name before the verb 'said' to report speech; از مهان uses از in the sense 'concerning/about the guests' (مهان = guests).
segments.s177-g192: همی here works as an emphatic/intonational particle ('indeed' / 'surely'). طوس is the subject (Tous, the hero), کم is a predicate adjective 'few/rare,' and باید is a modal 'must / ought' forming an impersonal statement; اندر جهان is the locative phrase 'in the world.' The clause expresses that Tous-like figures ought to be rare in the world.
segments.s177-g33: This clause ends with the past tense verb بیاراستند (third person plural), so the verb carries the action 'they adorned'. جهانی is the thing being adorned ('the whole world'), and به شادی is a prepositional phrase marking manner ('with/in joy'). The order is typical poetic Persian: object and adjuncts precede the verb; بیاراستند is an archaic/poetic past form of آراستن with plural ending.
segments.s177-g34: بهر جای is an old poetic prepositional phrase (بهر = for/for the sake of, جای = place) meaning 'for a place'. رامشگران is a plural agentive noun 'the peacemakers/comforters' governed by بهر جای, and خواستند (past 3rd pl of خواستن) is the main verb 'they desired/wanted'. The whole string means 'they desired a place for the peacemakers.'
segments.s177-g35: ازان پس is a compound adverbial 'after that / thereupon' (ازان = thereupon/of that, پس = after). ز marks origin ('from') and governs the noun phrase کشور مهان جهان = 'the country/realm of the world's guests' (کشور head, modified by مهان جهان). The phrase specifies where they came from.
segments.s177-g36: برفتند is past tense third person plural 'they went/they departed.' یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / all together / straight' modifying their action, سوی is the preposition 'toward', and اصفهان is the place name. Together: 'they departed straight toward Isfahan.'
segments.s177-g37: Here the verb بیاراست appears before the subject (poetic inversion): بیاراست is the past tense 'he/one adorned' and گودرز is the subject 'Goudarz'. کاخ بلند is the direct object 'a high/lofty palace' (noun + postpositive adjective). Such inversion is common in epic poetry.
segments.s177-g38: همه is a quantifier 'all/entire', placed before the noun دیبه (fine silk / silk robe) which is modified by خسروانی 'royal'. فگند is an archaic past plural form (from فکندن) 'they threw/they cast/they laid'. The verb is plural and the logical subject is supplied by the narrative context (those present).
segments.s177-g39: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain', here introducing the action. تخت is the direct object 'throne'. بنهاد is the past tense (third person singular) of نهادن 'he placed/laid/established' (an archaic poetic form). پیکر به زر is a noun phrase 'a figure/shape in/of gold' (پیکر = figure; به زر = 'in/with gold' marking material). The clause thus says he placed a throne and (on/with it) a golden figure.
segments.s177-g40: بدو = 'to it' (referring back to the throne/figure), اندرون 'inside/within' gives the location, and چند گونه گهر = 'several kinds of gems' (چند = several; گونه = kind/type; گهر = gem). The verb from the previous clause (بنهاد) is implicitly continued here: 'he put into it several types of jewels.'
segments.s177-g241: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning “when.” بودن here (باشد) is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive form of بودن and links the subject فرزند to the predicate نبیره; the noun کلاه is not inflected here because in this poetic order it stands ready as the direct object of the verb in the next segment (i.e. “put on the cap”). Note the inverted, compact word order typical of classical verse.
segments.s177-g242: چرا is a rhetorical question word “why.” The two verbs برنهد and برنشیند are parallel actions with the prefix بر- (on/onto): برنهد = “put on/place on” (here: put on the cap) and برنشیند = “sit down/take a seat.” به گاه is a locative phrase “at the time / on the occasion.” The verbs are in the same mood tense appropriate for a rhetorical question.
segments.s177-g243: بدو is the prepositional phrase “to him.” گفت is the past tense verb “said” (here used with بدو to mean “said to him”). گودرز is the speaker’s name, and کای is a vocative particle meaning “O” used to address the addressee; کم خرد is an adjectival phrase “of little wisdom / ill‑witted.” Word order places the indirect object phrase first (بدو گفت) as is common in classical style.
segments.s177-g244: ترا is the accusative form “you” (ت + را); را is the object marker attached here as part of ترا. بخرد is adjectival/qualitative “wise” (used comparatively in context). از introduces the standard Persian comparative construction “than” (از مردمان = than the people). نشمرد is a negative imperative/command form meaning “do not count/consider” (literally “do not reckon”), so the whole clause means “do not deem yourself wiser than the people.”
segments.s177-g245: به گیتی is a locative phrase “in the world.” کسی is the indefinite subject “anyone/someone” used here in a negative clause. چون is a comparative particle “like/as,” and نبود is the past negative copula “was not,” giving the sense “there was no one like Siavash.” Note the classical placement of the verb at the end.
segments.s177-g246: چنو (چنان‌و) introduces the following qualities in comparison (“such as”/“so”); راد و آزاد و خامش is a triad of adjectival qualities: راد “generous,” آزاد here as “noble/free in spirit,” and خامش “calm/quiet/gentle.” The line ends with نبود (past negative “was not”), so together: “no one was so generous, noble, and gentle.”
segments.s177-g247: کنون is “now.” این جهانجوی is a nominal phrase “this world‑seeking (one)” (i.e., an ambitious, worldly man). فرزند اوست is a nominal clause with the copula ست “is,” meaning “is his son.” Word order places the demonstrative + epithet before the predicate and finishes with the copula as typical of classical Persian.
segments.s177-g248: همویست functions as an emphatic copular/adjectival phrase meaning “is the same / is like him.” گویی here has its poetical sense (“one might say” / “as if”), and به چهر و به پوست are parallel prepositional phrases “in face and in skin,” i.e. “in appearance and in bodily features.” The line asserts strong physical resemblance.
segments.s177-g1: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when'. با + گیو marks accompaniment 'with Giv'. کیخسرو is the proper name (here the subject in the clause). آمد is simple past 'came'. به normally marks goal 'into/toward' (here introducing the following locative phrase). زم is an archaic/poetic item (here functioning as part of the locative 'into the world/land'); its exact classical form would be زمین; the poet uses a shortened/poetic form.
segments.s177-g2: جهان is the noun 'the world' (subject/topic). چند here means 'some, a portion' (indefinite quantity). ازو is the old enclitic/contracted form 'از او' meaning 'because of him / from him' and links the following predicate to Kay Khosrow. شاد is predicative adjective 'joyful'. و is the coordinating 'and'. چندی here is adverbial 'for a while / in part' and modifies the following adjective. دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim'. The clause reads as a contrast: parts of the world were joyful on his account and parts were sullen.
segments.s177-g3: نوندی (nawand) is a musical term or tune-name placed before the verb as the object/topic. به هر سو is an adverbial phrase 'to every side/everywhere' (به + هر + سو). برافگند is a past tense verb 'scattered/sent forth' (archaic poetic form of برافکند) and appears before the subject here (verb–subject order is common in classical Persian). گیو after the verb is the agent 'Giv' (subject).
segments.s177-g4: یکی نامه is an indefinite nominal phrase 'one letter / a certain letter' (یکی as indefinite numeral). از شاه is the source 'from the king'. وز here is a contracted/poetic combination equivalent to 'و ز' = 'and from' (an archaic linkage of the conjunction و and the preposition ز/از); the printed lexical senses given do not match this poetic usage, so it should be read functionally as 'and from'. گیو نیو yields the second source and thing: 'from Giv, a ney' (نیو = ney, reed‑flute); syntactically the two sources are coordinated: 'from the king and from Giv'.
segments.s177-g5: که introduces a relative/descriptive clause 'who/that'. آمد is past 'came'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. توران is the place name 'Turan' (source). جهاندار is a title/noun 'sovereign, lord of the world' used here as an appositive/title. شاد is an epithet 'joyful' describing that sovereign.
segments.s177-g6: سر تخمه is an idiomatic nominal phrase 'head of the stock/lineage' (سر = head/chief; تخمه = offspring/seed = lineage). نامور is an adjective 'renowned' modifying تخمه (the lineage). کیقباد is the personal name in apposition to the phrase — 'the famed scion, Kay Qobad.' The whole noun group names the famous head of that house.
segments.s177-g7: فرستاده بختیار is a noun phrase that can be read as 'the envoy Bakhtiyar' (فرستاده + proper name) or 'the envoy of Bakhtiyar'; context makes بختیار a name. و is the coordinating 'and'. سوار is 'rider/horseman' and is coordinated with the preceding noun phrase, producing a compound list of persons.
segments.s177-g8: This segment lists epithets: خردمند 'wise' is an adjectival epithet; و is the coordinating 'and'. بینادل is a rare/uncertain poetic word (the manuscript/lexicon reading is unclear) and here functions as another epithet; the precise meaning is uncertain. و 'and' again coordinates, and دوستدار is an adjectival noun 'friendly/attentive/devoted' describing the same person(s). Together they form a string of descriptors attached to the rider/envoy.
segments.s177-g129: کسی را که ... پهلوان introduces a relative clause: 'کسی را' (literally 'someone' with را as an emphatic/topic marker) is the head, and 'که' introduces the clause describing that someone. 'چون او بود پهلوان' literally orders the clause as 'like him was a champion' — 'چون' = 'like', 'او' = 'he', 'بود' = past copula 'was', and 'پهلوان' is the predicate noun 'champion'. The construction means 'anyone who was a champion like him'.
segments.s177-g130: بود جاودان شاد و روشن‌روان is a predicative phrase with the past copula 'بود' linking the subject of the previous clause to a list of adjectival predicates. The adjectives 'جاودان' (eternal), 'شاد' (happy) and the compound adjective 'روشن‌روان' (literally 'bright‑soul', a poetic term meaning cheerful/bright‑spirited) follow the copula and describe the subject.
segments.s177-g131: یکی کاخ کشواد بد در صطخر: 'یکی' = 'a certain/one' (introducing an item), 'کاخ کشواد' = 'the palace of Keshvad' (noun + proper name). In this poetic line 'بد' functions as an archaic/metrical variant of the copula 'بود' (i.e. 'there was' / 'stood'); 'در صطخر' gives the location 'in Istakhr' with 'در' as the locative preposition.
segments.s177-g132: که آزادگان را بدو بود فخر is a relative clause modifying the palace. 'که' = 'which/that'; 'آزادگان را' = 'the nobles' marked by را (here marking the experiencer/concerned group); 'بدو' = 'in him/to him' (dative pronoun referring back to the palace/Keshvad); 'بود' = past copula; 'فخر' = predicate noun 'pride' — literal: 'that for the nobles there was pride in him', i.e. 'which was a pride to the nobles because of him.'
segments.s177-g133: چو از تخت کاووس برخاستند: 'چو' = 'when'; 'از' = 'from'; 'تخت کاووس' = 'Kavus's throne' (noun + name, genitive relation). 'برخاستند' is the past plural verb 'they rose/rose up/rose from' and gives the main action of the temporal clause.
segments.s177-g134: به ایوان نو رفتن آراستند: 'به ایوان نو' = 'to the new iwan' (locative phrase). Here 'رفتن' appears as the verbal noun/infinitive 'going' tied to the main verb 'آراستند' (they adorned/prepared), so the clause reads as they proceeded to the new iwan and adorned (it/itself) — 'آراستند' is past plural, and the phrase is slightly elliptical/poetic in its incorporation of the infinitive.
segments.s177-g135: همی رفت گودرز با شهریار: 'همی' is an adverb signaling continuous or ongoing action ('was still/kept'), 'رفت' = 'went/was going' (past), 'گودرز' is the subject proper name, and 'با شهریار' = 'with the king' (prepositional phrase). The line sets the scene of them proceeding together.
segments.s177-g136: چو آمد بدان گلشن زرنگار: 'چو' = 'when'; 'آمد' = 'came' (past). The sequence 'ب' + 'دان' here functions as a poetic compound meaning 'to that' (i.e. 'to that place'); 'گلشن' = 'garden'; 'زرنگار' = an adjectival compound 'gilded/embroidered with gold' (poetic epithet). The whole segment means 'when (they) came to that gilded garden.'
segments.s177-g233: بدو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning 'to him' placed before the verb; گفت is the simple past third‑person singular 'said' (classical narrative), and شاه‌ای خردمند پیر is arranged as subject + vocative/epithet: شاه (the king) is the speaker and ای + خردمند پیر functions as the address/appositive 'O wise old man' following the verb.
segments.s177-g234: منه here is an archaic negative imperative ('do not' / 'do not put'), followed by the noun phrase زهر برنده 'biting/acute poison' (زهر noun + برنده adjective after it). بر جام شیر is a prepositional phrase 'on/in the cup of milk' where بر (= 'on/into') governs جام ('cup') which is qualified by شیر ('milk').
segments.s177-g235: بنه here is an imperative/archaic verbal form meaning something like 'set/put' or 'lay down' (classical usage), followed by تیغ 'sword'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بگشای is an imperative 'open/unsheathe' (2nd singular), and ز آهن میان is a small prepositional phrase: ز ('from/of') + آهن ('iron') + میان ('between/in the midst'), specifying from what or where.
segments.s177-g236: نباید is an impersonal modal expression 'one must not/it ought not', کزین is a fused prepositional compound ('from this' = ک+از+این), سود is 'benefit/profit' as the grammatical subject of دارد, دارد is the present indicative 'has' (here 'there is' or 'brings'), and زیان is the predicate nominal 'harm/loss' — together: 'it must not be that from this benefit there is harm'.
segments.s177-g237: چنین is a formulaic adverb 'thus/so'. گفت again is the past‑tense verb 'said' (narrative past). طوس سپهبد is the speaker's name plus title (Tūs, the spahbed); به شاه marks the indirect object 'to the king' — word order is formulaic: adverb + verb + agent + title + indirect object.
segments.s177-g238: که‌گر is a fused conditional 'که گر' meaning 'if'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. سیر آید is a two‑word predicate: سیر ('sated') plus آید ('comes'/'becomes') = 'becomes sated/comes to be satiated'. از تخت و گاه uses از + تخت ('from the throne') and و گاه ('and the court/time'), listing what the king might grow weary of; آید here is present/future sense used in the conditional.
segments.s177-g239: به فرزند باید is an impersonal/obligative construction: به فرزند ('for the son' or 'to his son') + باید ('it is necessary' / 'ought') introduces a clause with که + ماند as result. که is the subordinating conjunction 'that', and ماند ('remain') is present/subjunctive sense 'be left/continue', with جهان ('the world') its direct object or what should remain.
segments.s177-g240: This is a nominal list (apposition) specifying what should remain: بزرگی ('greatness') + و + دیهیم (here a classical term referring to the rural holdings/people or lands associated with the realm) + و تخت مهان ('and the royal/guestly throne' where تخت is 'throne' modified by مهان). The structure is simply a coordination of nouns appositional to جهان.
segments.s177-g249: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. از تور دارد ز مادر نژاد literally orders as 'has from Tur (تور) lineage from (ز) mother' — the verb دارد (has) is present tense 'has/possesses' and the prepositional phrases 'از تور' and 'ز مادر' mark origin (maternal Turanian ancestry). Word order is typical classical/poetic Persian: condition marker + prepositional origin phrases + possession verb + noun 'نژاد' (lineage).
segments.s177-g250: هم here functions as 'even/still' (emphatic). از تخم شاهی = 'from the seed/stock of kingship' (تخم = seed/origin; شاهی = kingship). نپیچد is a negative present/subjunctive or jussive form (ن‑ + پیچد) meaning 'is not/shall not be turned aside' or 'will not be cut off' — the negating ن is attached directly to the verb, a common classical/poetic formation. ز داد 'by justice' gives the instrumental/causal force: justice will not turn him away from royal seed.
segments.s177-g251: به توران و ایران sets the domain 'in/among Turan and Iran' (به = 'in/among' here). چنو (چنان + و variant) = 'such / like so' and نیو is a poetic noun (here 'ney'/'reed' image) or epithet modifying the implied person — the clause ends with کی ست 'who is it?' (کی = who; ست = is). Word order places the locative phrase first for emphasis, then the simile/adjective and the copula question.
segments.s177-g252: چنین خام گفتارت = 'such naive/immature is your speech' (چنین = thus/so; خام = raw/naive; گفتارت = 'your speech' with the possessive -ت). از بهر چیست is a stock classical question phrase 'for what purpose is it?' (از بهر = 'for the sake of', چی = 'what?', ست = 'is'). The sentence is a reproach: adjective + possessed noun, then purpose-question.
segments.s177-g253: دو چشمت 'your two eyes' is a possessive noun (چشمت = 'your eye/eyes'). نبیند is the negative present/future sense 'will not see' (ن‑ prefix with بیند from بین = to see) and همی here gives continuative/emphatic sense 'ever/at all' — so the clause reads 'your two eyes will not (ever) see his face'. Word order is subject (possessed noun) + verb + object.
segments.s177-g254: چنان برز و بالا = 'so/high and exalted' (چنان = such/so; برز = high/exalted; بالا = high/lofty). و آن مهر او is a nominative/identifying phrase 'and that مهر of his' — مهر here is a poetic noun (lit. 'love/affection' or 'favour') and is described by the preceding adjectives. The clause is verbless (typical of Persian descriptive lines): adjectives precede the noun they qualify for emphasis.
segments.s177-g255: به جیحون گذر کرد = 'he passed/crossed the Jeyhun' — گذر کرد is a compound verb (گذَر + کرد) meaning 'made the crossing / passed'. و کشتی نجست mirrors a parallel classical phrasing: نجست is a past-form of جست (here with the negative-prefix sense 'did not seek/take'), so کشتی نجست means 'he did not take / did not go by ship.' The clause uses simple past narrative tense common in epic Persian.
segments.s177-g256: به فر کیانی و رای درست is a verbless, attributive phrase describing manner/quality: به فر کیانی = 'with Kayanian (royal) glory' (فر = royal radiance/glory; کیانی = Kayanian/royal), و رای درست = 'and with right/sound counsel' (رای = counsel/plan; درست = correct/proper). The preposition به introduces the sphere/means (with/by), and the two nouns joined by و give parallel attributes.
segments.s177-g273: بدو (bedo) is the prepositional pronoun 'to him / to you' placed before the verb; گفت is the verb 'said' in past tense; گودرز is the subject (the name of the speaker). چندین is an adverb meaning 'many / repeatedly' modifying the following negative imperative مگوی (مگو + ی), which is the 2nd-person negative imperative 'do not say / do not tell.' Word order is topical: 'To him said Goudarz: do not say many (things).'
segments.s177-g274: که introduces the clause of purpose/reason ('lest / so that'), چندین repeats the idea 'again and again' or 'many times', نبینم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive negative 'that I may not see' (ن + بینم), and ت + را together form the object 'you' with the definite/object marker (تِرا). آب روی is a compound: آب literally 'water' (here figurative: tears) + روی 'face' = 'tearful face / tear-stained countenance.' The clause therefore gives the reason: 'lest I repeatedly see your tearful face.'
segments.s177-g275: به + name + گفت is the standard order 'said to Kavus' (به کاووس گفت). ای is the vocative particle 'O', followed by two titles in apposition جهاندار شاه 'sovereign king' addressing Kavus respectfully.
segments.s177-g276: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' (emphatic/explicit), دل را marks 'the heart' as the object with را, and مگردان is a negative imperative 'do not turn/turn away' (مگردان = مَگَردان). ز is the poetic variant of از meaning 'from', and آیین و راه are the objects 'custom / tradition and way / path' — together: 'do not turn your heart away from custom and the right way.'
segments.s177-g277: دو فرزند is the numeral + noun 'two children/sons'; پرمایه is an epithet meaning 'of substance / of worth' (here 'noble' or 'well-endowed'), and را marks them as the object (the ones to be presented). پیش خوان literally 'before the table' (پیش = in the presence of / before, خوان = banquet/table) — a phrase meaning 'presented at the feast / brought before the royal table.' The whole segment names who should be presented.
segments.s177-g278: سزاوار is the predicate adjective 'worthy / deserving'; گاهند here functions as a verbal/categorical copular phrase (poetically contracted) giving state — 'they are of the place / they are fit for the station' (i.e. 'are worthy'). و هر دو جوان adds 'and both are young' (هر دو = both; جوان = young). The clause therefore states their fitness and youth.
segments.s177-g279: ببین is the imperative 'look / see'. تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that', ز is the poetic 'of/from', هر دو 'each of the two / both', and سزاوار 'deserving'. کیست is the interrogative/relative 'who is it' (کی + ست = who is). Together the clause means 'look, so as to see which of the two is deserving.'
segments.s177-g280: که here heads the relative/explanatory clause 'who' / 'for he who'. با introduces accompanying qualities 'with', برز denotes exaltation/high rank (here an abstract quality), و repeats 'and', با فره names 'the farr' (فره = divine glory or royal splendor), and ایزدیست is an adjectival/nominal form meaning 'is divine / godly' — altogether: 'who has both elevation and divine farr.' The clause explains what makes one 'سزاوار' above.
segments.s177-g297: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The verb بشنید (past) appears before its subject — a common classical inversion — so the literal order is 'when heard Goudarz and Tous this speech'. بشنید is the simple past (they/he heard) with the plural subject expressed after it. این سخن = 'this/the foregoing speech/words' is the object/topic of their hearing.
segments.s177-g298: که is the complementizer/relative 'that' introducing the content of the words. افگند is the simple past (3rd person) of افکندن 'to cast/throw' and here reports what was done/uttered; the verb again precedes any following noun phrase. سالار is a title 'commander/chief' modifying or appositional to هشیار (a personal name/title). بن appears immediately after the name and is left syntactically unresolved here (it can be a substantive 'base/bottom' in the lexicon given), so the clause gives the agent/title and a following noun without completing the larger clause in this line.
segments.s177-g299: برین is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + این) meaning 'on/at this'. هر دو 'both' is the subject; گشتند is the past plural of گشتن meaning 'became' (they became); همداستان is a predicate adjective 'of one mind / like-minded'. Word order follows classical style: prepositional phrase, subject, verb, predicate.
segments.s177-g300: ندانست is the negative past (ن + دانست) literally 'did not know/consider', here used idiomatically to mean they did not reveal or make known. ازین (ز + این in poetic spelling) marks the topic/source 'of this/about this'; به کسی places an indirect object 'to anyone'; داستان is the thing not told — 'the story/matter'. The clause thus says they did not make this matter known to anyone.
segments.s177-g301: برین (بر + این) 'on this' again opens the clause. یک سخن 'one speech' is the direct object: 'a single statement'. دل here qualifies the manner—'from the heart / heartfelt'—and بیاراستند is past plural 'they adorned/arranged/put in order', used idiomatically as 'they prepared/phrased' (i.e., they composed a heartfelt speech). The verb is plural agreeing with the earlier subjects.
segments.s177-g302: ز پیش here means 'from before / in the presence of' (poetic z for از + پیش). جهاندار is the sovereign/king and stands as the locative/personal reference 'before the sovereign'. برخاستند is past plural 'they rose/stood up (and approached/rose to speak)'. The phrase order places the prepositional/time phrase before the verb as is common in the epic style.
segments.s177-g303: چو = 'when'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. برزد is an archaic past form (here meaning 'rose/appeared/lifted'); classical Persian often places the verb before or near the subject. سر 'head' with از برج شیر 'from the tower of the Lion' (the zodiacal/astral image) indicates the sun raising its head from that sign or tower.
segments.s177-g304: سپهر 'the sky/heavens' is the subject of the verb phrase اندر آورد (an older compound meaning 'brought in/caused/put down'). شب را uses را to mark 'the night' as the definite object; به زیر 'beneath/under' states the result/place. Together: the heavens put the night beneath (i.e., dispelled or pushed down the night).
segments.s177-g257: بسان فریدون introduces a simile ('like Fereydun'). کز is the relative particle ('who/that') linking Fereydun to the following relative clause; اروند رود is the noun phrase 'Arvand river' (name + noun), with the river-name followed by the common noun رود.
segments.s177-g258: گذشت is a simple past verb 'he passed'. The conjunction و ('and') connects the two actions. به کشتی نیامد فرود is a negative past of the compound verb فرود آمدن ('to descend/land'), expressed poetically with فرود placed after نیامد: 'did not come down into the ship.' به marks direction ('into/towards' the ship).
segments.s177-g259: ز here functions as a causal preposition 'because of / by reason of'. مردی is the abstract noun 'manliness, bravery' and is given as the first cause; و links it to از فره ایزدی, where از + فره ایزدی means 'from/of the divine farr' (فره 'farr', ایزدی qualifies it as 'divine').
segments.s177-g260: ازو دور شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'was removed from him / turned away from him' (literally 'from-him distant became' — شد is the past of شدن 'to become' used here as resultative). چشم و دست بدی is the delayed subject phrase 'the evil eye and hand' (بدی adjectival/attributive 'evil') that the verb applies to; poetic word order places the verb earlier and the subject after it.
segments.s177-g261: تو is the emphatic/explicit second-person subject. نوذر نژادی is a genitive phrase 'of Nowzar's lineage/stock' (نژادی = 'by descent, of that race'). نه negates the following predicate; بیگانه‌ای is the predicative 'you are a stranger' (the copula is omitted in poetic style).
segments.s177-g262: پدر تیز بود is a complete past-clause 'your father was keen/sharp' with بود as the past copula. و links to the second clause; تو دیوانه‌ای is a present/predicative clause 'you are mad' expressed with the adjectival form دیوانه plus the enclitic/poetic ending ای (vocative/exclamatory) — the copula is again implicit or archaically expressed.
segments.s177-g263: سلیح من = 'my weapon.' ار is the conditional particle 'if.' با + منستی here expresses possession/being with me in an archaic/poetic form (منستی = 'it were mine / were with me'); کنون is the adverb 'now.' The whole segment sets a counterfactual condition ('If my weapon were with me now').
