segments.s177-g264: بر و یالت is a paired body-image phrase 'brow (بر) and your mane (یالت)' serving as the object that would be affected. آغشته گشتی is a poetic resultative: آغشته 'soaked/tinged' plus گشتی used resultatively 'became/got' (together: 'would become soaked'). به خون marks the instrument/material 'with blood.' Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (the verb/auxiliary follows the adjective).
segments.s177-g97: مرا (م + را) is the object 'me' placed before the verb; چند is an adverb meaning 'somewhat / a little'; ببسود is an older/poetic verb form (with the b- prefix) meaning 'was of use / did good'; و simply links the two clauses; چندی بگفت = 'he said a little / spoke for a short while' with بگفت as simple past. Note the shift of subjects between clauses is normal in narrative poetry.
segments.s177-g98: This clause is centered on کردم (first-person past 'I did/made') as the main verb; خرد (here 'wisdom') is the theme/object, با هنر is an instrumental phrase 'with skill', and اندر نهفت is a small clause meaning 'hid within / concealed inside' (اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hidden/concealed'). The verb کردم governs the following locative/result phrase.
segments.s177-g99: بترسیدم is first‑person past 'I feared / I was afraid'; از introduces the cause/source ('of/from') and links to the coordinated nouns کار و کردار 'work/deeds and conduct/actions'; او after those nouns is the pronoun marking possession ('his'), placed after the nouns in this classical order.
segments.s177-g100: بپیچیدم is first‑person past (literal 'I turned' / figuratively 'I turned away, was perplexed'); از again marks cause ('because of / from'); رنج و تیمار او 'his suffering and care' are the coordinated causes, with او following them to indicate 'his'. The clause expresses result or reaction to the previous fear.
segments.s177-g101: اگر introduces a conditional clause; ویژه modifies the following quality (here 'especially' or 'special'); ابری is adjectival 'cloudy' (or referring to clouds); شود is third‑person present/subjunctive 'becomes/should become'; در بار is a locative/temporal phrase 'in the rain' (بار here = rainfall). The clause sets a hypothetical scene.
segments.s177-g102: کشنده پدر is a noun phrase 'the killer of the father' (کشنده as noun 'killer' with پدر following to mark the victim/possessed); چون here is interrogative 'how'; بود is past 'was' (here used to form the question 'how was ...'); دوستدار is predicate 'a friend / fond of' — together: 'how was the father's killer a friend?'.
segments.s177-g103: نخواند is the negative past third person 'did not read/recite' (the poetic biconsonantal form with negative); مرا is the object 'me' split as م + را in the word list; موبد is the subject 'the mobad / Zoroastrian priest'; از آب پاک is a prepositional phrase 'from/of pure water' indicating the ritual of purification. Word order reflects classical literary placement (verb before subject).
segments.s177-g104: که introduces a purpose/explanatory clause ('so that / in order that'); بپرستم is first‑person present/subjunctive 'that I may worship / revere'; او را is the object pronoun 'him' marked by را; پدر is an appositive 'father' identifying that him as father; زیر خاک is a locative 'under the earth' (i.e., 'beneath the ground').
segments.s177-g201: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. گودرز را is the definite object (name + object marker را) of دید ('saw; beheld'). The conjunction و links that clause to the following noun phrase; چندان سپاه is a noun phrase meaning 'so great a host' (چندان = 'to that extent / so great', سپاه = 'army, host').
segments.s177-g202: کزو ('from which / from whom') refers back to the large host just mentioned; تیره شد is a compound predicate 'grew dark / became dark' (تیره = dark, شد = became). روی خورشید و ماه = 'the faces of the sun and moon' (روی 'face' precedes the two celestial nouns). The whole line reads literally 'from which became dark the face of sun and moon.'
segments.s177-g203: یکی 'one / a certain' introduces the noun تخت 'throne'. بر + کوهه is the locative phrase 'upon a mound / hill'. ژنده پیل is a compound epithet 'a ragged (ژنده) elephant (پیل)' — poetically the elephant is compared to a mountain (huge); the prepositional phrase attaches to تخت: 'a throne upon a (mountain‑like) ragged elephant.'
segments.s177-g204: ز (poetic 'از') here marks material or appearance: 'of / from'. پیروزه تابان = 'shining turquoise' (پیروزه = turquoise stone/color, تابان = shining). به کردار is an idiomatic simile 'in the manner/appearance of', and نیل here means the deep blue/indigo hue. Together: 'made of / gleaming with turquoise, in appearance like indigo.'
segments.s177-g205: This is a stacked nominal phrase: جهانجوی ('world‑seeking', an epithet) modifies the name کیخسرو (Kaykhosrow), followed by the epithet تاج‌ور ('crown‑bearer' / crowned). These epithets frame the king's character and status before the predicate.
segments.s177-g206: نشسته is the perfect‑participle used predicatively 'seated'. بران = بر + ان (that) = 'on that (thereon)', referring back to the throne; تخت is the noun 'throne'. و links the following participial phrase; بسته کمر is a compact adjectival phrase meaning 'girdle‑fastened' or 'with his belt tied' (بسته 'bound/tied', کمر 'waist / girdle'), the possessive is implicit from the subject.
segments.s177-g207: به گرد اندرش is a prepositional phrase 'around him' (به گرد = around/about, اندر + ـش = 'in/at him' → 'around him'). ژنده‌پیلان is the plural 'ragged elephants' and is counted by دویست (two hundred) following it in this poetic inversion (numeral placed after the noun for metrical/orderly reasons).
segments.s177-g208: تو گفتی is a reporting phrase literally 'you said' but here functions idiomatically as a general report ('people/one said' or 'it was said'); گفتی is the verbal/narrative element. به گیتی = 'in the world'; جز آن جای نیست = 'there is no place except that' (جز = except, آن جای = that place, نیست = 'is not' / 'there is not'). The clause is a quoted or reported judgement about uniqueness.
segments.s177-g137: بر is a preposition 'on/upon' placed before the noun phrase; اورنگ زرینش is a noun phrase 'the golden throne' where زرینش is زرین + enclitic -ش = 'his golden (one)'; بنشاندند is past, 3rd person plural of بنشاندن (causative of نشستن) meaning 'they seated' or 'they set (him) on'. The direct object 'him' is understood from the possessive -ش on زرین.
segments.s177-g138: برو here functions as a discourse/poetic particle ('thereupon') rather than a literal imperative 'go' (the provided senses don't include this literary use). بر marks the target ('upon him'); بسی is an intensifier 'much/many' (an older usage); آفرین is a noun 'acclamation/bravo'; خواندند is past 3pl 'they uttered/they cried' — together 'they uttered many acclaims.'
segments.s177-g139: ببستند is past 3rd plural 'they bound/tied/girded'; گردان ایران is a genitive phrase 'the troop/force of Iran' (گردان = troop, ایران = the Iranian force/people); کمر here denotes 'waist/girdle' (i.e., they girded their loins), so the clause means 'they girded the Iranian troops (for battle).' Word order puts the verb before its object, typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s177-g140: بجز introduces an exception 'except'; طوس and نوذر are proper names (Tūs and Nowzar). که begins a relative clause 'who/that' referring to the last-mentioned person; پیچید سر is a verbal phrase 'he turned (پیچید) his head (سر)' — an idiom meaning he averted/bowed his head or withdrew. The relative clause modifies the preceding name(s).
segments.s177-g141: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; او بود is 'he was' (بود as past copula); با + noun phrase 'با کوس و زرینه کفش' means 'with a war-drum and golden shoes' (زرینه is an adjective 'golden' modifying کفش). The coordinator و links the two items in the instrumental phrase.
segments.s177-g142: هم is an emphatic/also particle 'indeed/also'; او داشتی is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'he had' (داشتی = had); کاویانی درفش is a noun phrase 'the Kayanian (Kavian) banner' (کاویانی as adjective/national/ dynastic epithet + درفش 'standard/banner'). The clause asserts his possession of that banner.
segments.s177-g143: ازان کار is an adverbial phrase 'from/after that affair'; گودرز شد is 'Goudarz became' (شد = become); تیز مغز is a compound predicate adjective 'sharp‑brained' (تیز = sharp, مغز = brain) meaning 'quick-witted'. The whole segment means 'Goudarz became sharp-witted because of that event.'
segments.s177-g144: بر او marks the indirect object 'to/for him'; پیامی فرستاد is 'sent a message' (پیامی = a message, فرستاد = he sent, past); نغز is an attributive adjective placed after the verb phrase for poetic effect meaning 'witty/pithy', so the clause reads 'he sent him a witty message.'
segments.s177-g161: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. بشنید is a past 3rd-person singular verb 'he heard' (classical simple past). پاسخ (object) precedes the verb, and چنین داد is a light idiom 'gave thus / answered thus' where داد is the past 'gave' and طوس (the subject) appears after the verb, a common flexible order in epic/Persian poetry.
segments.s177-g162: که introduces reported speech or a content clause ('that ...'). بر ما is a prepositional phrase 'for us' (بر used in a dative/benefactive sense). نه خوبست is the negated stative predicate 'is not good' (خوبست = 'it is good'); کردن فسوس is an infinitival complement where کردن is the light verb 'to do/engage in' attached to the noun فسوس ('whispering/idle talk'), so the clause means 'that for us it is not good to do/engage in فسوس.'
segments.s177-g163: به ایران places the scene 'in/at Iran' (به marking location/context). پس از is the compound expression 'after' (پس followed by از + name), so پس از رستم پیلتن = 'after Rostam of Piltan' with پیلتن an epithet/place-name following the personal name in the usual Persian sequence.
segments.s177-g164: سرافرازتر is a comparative adjective 'more exalted / more honored.' The clause uses the pattern 'سرافرازتر کس منم' where کس = 'anyone' and منم = 'I am' (present copula fused to pronoun), so the meaning is 'I am more honored than anyone.' ز انجمن ('from/among the assembly') marks the domain or group being compared; z functions poetically as 'among/of.'
segments.s177-g165: نبیره is a noun 'scion/descendant' that takes its possessor after it, so نبیره منوچهر = 'a descendant of Manuchehr.' شاه دلیر is an appositive 'the brave king' modifying Manuchehr; classical Persian commonly strings such nominal appositives without a copula.
segments.s177-g166: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') modifying the preceding king. گیتی is the theme/object 'the world.' به تیغ is an instrumental phrase 'by the sword' with به marking the instrument. اندر آورد زیر is the verbal construction: اندر (in/into) + آورد (past 'brought') + زیر ('under'), giving the sense 'brought under' or 'placed beneath' — i.e. 'put the world under the sword.'
segments.s177-g167: همان شیر is a demonstrative + noun 'that same lion.' پرخاشجویم is an adjectival predicate with the 1st-person ending -یم attached to the adjective 'پرخاشجو' meaning 'I am aggressive/quarrelsome' (in epic Persian adjective + personal ending expresses 'I am ...'). به جنگ is a locative/instrumental phrase 'in battle.'
segments.s177-g168: بدرم is a first-person verb 'I tear/rend' (classical form with b- prefix used for present/habitual or emphatic action). The objects 'دل پیل و چنگ پلنگ' are two coordinated noun phrases 'the elephant's heart and the leopard's claw' (possessive relation is understood by juxtaposition), so the clause boasts 'I tear the elephant's heart and the leopard's claw.'
segments.s177-g113: گمانی نبردم is a fixed idiom: گمانی (a supposition) + بردن (to take/hold) yields 'to suppose'; نبردم is the negative past 'I did not suppose'. که introduces the subordinate clause that spells out what was not supposed; هرگز (never) immediately follows که to qualify the subordinate verb. نهنگ is the subject of that subordinate clause.
segments.s177-g114: ز دریا is a prepositional source phrase 'from the sea'. بران سان is an archaic-adverbial combination (بران + سان) meaning 'in that manner/from there as' or 'so'; برآید is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive-ish narrative verb 'rises/comes forth'. به جنگ indicates direction/purpose 'into battle'.
segments.s177-g115: ازان پس که is a temporal connector 'after that, when'. پیران here functions as the coming person (in the Shahnameh it is the name Piran); بیامد is simple past 'came'. چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' modifying the ensuing action.
segments.s177-g116: میان بسته is a verbal phrase formed by میان (middle) + بسته (closed/tight) meaning 'he closed in on the middle' or 'grasped/locked in the center'. و links to the next phrase; بادپایی is an epithet 'the swift-footed (one)'. به زیر = 'under' (literally 'to under') indicates that the swift-footed person was put beneath/under him.
segments.s177-g117: به آب اندر آمد is a classical word order meaning 'went/entered into the water' (به + noun + اندر آمد). بسان نهنگ is a simile 'like a whale' placed after the verb to compare the action to that creature.
segments.s177-g118: که گفتی introduces a relative clause 'which you said/that you spoke of' (گفتی here functions as 'you said'). زمین را is the definite object with the object marker را. بسوزد is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'would burn/set on fire', and به جنگ ('in/at war') marks the occasion or means — 'burn the earth in battle'.
segments.s177-g119: بینداخت is a simple past 'he threw' (cast). بر یال او is the prepositional phrase 'upon his mane' (بر + یال + possessive او). The second بر before کمند shows the target of the throw: 'he cast the lasso onto/over the mane' (classical doubling of بر marks placement/target).
segments.s177-g120: سر پهلوان is the noun phrase 'the hero's/champion's head' (head + epithet). اندر آمد (literally 'came in') describes being brought into a state or place; به بند means 'into the bond/fetter' — i.e. 'into the noose' or 'caught in the lasso'. The overall sense: the hero's head ended up trapped by the noose.
segments.s177-g121: بخواهش‌گری is a verbal-noun/nominal phrase meaning 'by entreaty' (used adverbially). رفتم is past 1st-person ('I went/came'); Persian often places such adverbial noun phrases before the verb. ای شهریار is a vocative ('O king') addressing the addressee and follows the main clause.
segments.s177-g122: وگرنه = 'otherwise' introduces the counterfactual. بکندی is an archaic/poetic past/optative form meaning 'would (have) done' (subject omitted but understood from context). سرش را = سر + enclitic ش ('his') plus object marker را, marking 'his head' as definite object. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and بار here = 'load, burden'.
segments.s177-g123: بدان is the imperative of دانستن ('know'); here it functions as 'be aware' or 'know that'. کاو is an archaic/poetic element (classical 'کاو' ≈ 'که او' = 'that he') and links the imperative to the clause about the person. ز درد پدر is a prepositional phrase 'from his father's pain/suffering'. خسته بود = 'was weary' (بود is past copula).
segments.s177-g124: ز = 'because of' (gives cause). بد گفتن is a compound: بد modifies the gerund/infinitive گفتن ('ill-speaking' or 'speaking badly'). ما is the agent 'our'. زبان‌بسته is a compound adjective 'tight-lipped / silent'. بود is the past copula ('was'). Together: 'he was silent because of our ill-speaking.'
segments.s177-g125: چنین = 'thus/so' (adverbial). تا = 'until/up to'. لب رود جیحون is a genitive phrase 'the bank (لب) of the river (رود) Jeyhun' (classical word order places لب before رود جیحون). به جنگ attaches the notion 'in battle/fighting' (i.e. 'in the fight up to the riverbank').
segments.s177-g126: نیاسود is negative past (نی- + آسود) meaning 'did not rest/was not at peace' (here 'did not rest' in battle). با = 'with'. گرزه = 'mace/cudgel' and گاورنگ is an adjective or epithet 'tawny/ox-colored' modifying گرزه; adjective follows the noun in Persian.
segments.s177-g127: سرانجام = 'at last/finally'. بگذاشت is the past form of گذاشت with the prefixed b- (classical/poetic usage) and here means 'gave (himself) up to / allowed' — with the following phrase it means 'yielded'. جیحون is the subject (the river/personified) and به خشم = 'to anger/in anger' indicates the state he gave way to.
segments.s177-g128: بآب و بکشتی are contracted poetic forms of به آب و به کشتی ('to/toward the water and to the ship'); the initial b- is the classical allomorph of به. نیفگند is negative past of افکندن ('did not throw'); used with چشم ('eye') it means 'did not cast (a) glance'. The clause therefore says he did not look toward the water or the ship.
segments.s177-g193: This is a classical Persian SOV-style clause where نبیره is the subject (‘a scion/descendant’), پسر is the direct object (‘sons’) and داشت is the past-tense verb ‘had’. The numeral phrase هفتاد و هشت (literally “seventy and eight”) names the quantity of the object; in older/poetic Persian numerals can follow the verb or be written with و between tens and units.
segments.s177-g194: Two coordinated past actions with the same implied subject: بزد (past, ‘he struck’) takes کوس as its object (the war‑drum). The following prepositional phrase ز ایوان به میدان (with ز = “from” and به = “to/into”) governs گذشت (past ‘he passed’). Word order is slightly elevated/poetic but straightforward: Verb–Object then a motion phrase + verb.
segments.s177-g195: سواران (plural) is the noun ‘horsemen/riders’ modified by the adjective جنگی ‘warlike/military’. The numeral cluster ده و دو هزار (classical poetic rendering of the number) follows the noun; و here links parts of the numeral (ten and two). This is a compressed way of giving the count of the horsemen.
segments.s177-g196: برون‌رفت functions here as the verbal idea ‘they went/rode out’ (a poetic compound), followed by برگستوان‌ور, an adjectival/epithetic compound meaning ‘mighty‑armed’ or ‘powerful in the arm(s)’, and then سوار as a reinforcing noun ‘riders’. The sequence describes the action (rode out) and character (mighty‑armed riders).
segments.s177-g197: وزان is an adverbial/relational particle ‘from that (place/time/side)’, رو here indicates the ‘side/direction/face’ (hence ‘from that side’), and بیامد is the past tense ‘came’. سپهدار is a title ‘commander’ in apposition to the proper name طوس that follows. The clause reads ‘from that side came the commander Tūs.’
segments.s177-g198: ببستند is past plural ‘they bound/tied/fastened’ (used transitively). The preposition بر = ‘on/upon’ introduces the place phrase کوهه پیل (literally ‘hill/mountain of the elephant’ — a compact genitive/attributive phrase without an explicit ezāfe in poetry). The final word کوس is the object ‘the drum’. Poetic word order places the place-phrase before the object and after the verb.
segments.s177-g199: ببستند here carries the sense ‘they drew up/closed (their ranks)’, followed by گردان ایران ‘the Iranian division/troop’ (گردان = unit/troop, ایران = Iran/the Iranians). میان (‘in the middle/in the midst’) indicates the placement of that unit. The verb precedes its object in this poetic line, but the meaning is ‘they placed/formed the Iranian troop in the middle.’
segments.s177-g200: به پیش is a compound prepositional notion ‘before/in front of’; سپاه is the governed noun ‘army/host’. اختر is an epithet literally ‘star/bright one’, here qualifying the following نام/epithet, and کاویان names the Kayanian dynasty/line — together marking the force as the Kayanian host. The whole phrase means ‘in front of the Kayanian host.’
segments.s177-g305: This is a compact list of companions introduced by با (with). In poetry Persian often lists names without conjunctions; فریبرز is the first name (subject of the following verb), با marks accompaniment, and طوس نوذر دمان are the names placed after it as coordinated companions. Word order is name + با + other names, rather than the prose order that might put با between names or use و.
segments.s177-g306: به نزدیک شاه آمدند آن زمان is an adverbial phrase + verb + temporal phrase. به نزدیک + شاه = `near the king/towards the king`; آمدند is the 3rd‑person plural past (here narrative) meaning “they came/approached”; آن زمان is a temporal phrase ‘at that time’ (آن = then, زمان = time). The plural verb agrees with the group named in the previous segment.
segments.s177-g307: In poetic word order the reportive phrase چنين گفت (lit. ‘said thus’) comes first, followed by the addressee phrase با شاه هشیار (to/with the wise/alert king), and the speaker’s name طوس appears at the end as the agent. هشیار is an adjective modifying شاه. The sequence should be read: طوس چنین گفت به شاه هشیار (Tūs said thus to the alert king).
segments.s177-g308: که introduces the content of what was said (‘that’ / ‘namely’). من is the 1st‑person subject. با سپهبد marks an accompanying person (‘with the spahbed’). برم is an archaic/persianized present/subjunctive form from بردن (here expressing intended action: ‘I will carry/bring’). پیل و کوس are the direct objects (elephants and war‑drums); و simply coordinates them.
segments.s177-g309: همان here is emphatic (‘the very / myself’), followed by the explicit subject من. کشم is a 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive form (from کشیدن) expressing an intended action ‘I will raise/hoist’; in poetry the verb appears directly after the subject. کاویانی درفش is a compound noun phrase ‘the Kayanian banner/standard’ (کاویانی = Kayanian/royal, درفش = banner).
segments.s177-g310: This clause gives the result/action: رخ لعل دشمن کنم چون بنفش. رخ لعل is a noun phrase ‘a face/cheek of ruby color’ (the adjective/descriptor follows the noun in poetry without an explicit ezāfe). دشمن is the affected party ‘the enemy’ (understood as ‘the enemy’s face’). کنم is 1st‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used causatively ‘I will make/bring about’. چون introduces a simile ‘like/as’, and بنفش is the color ‘purple’. The whole line means: ‘I will make the enemy’s face ruby‑red, like purple.’
segments.s177-g311: کنون and همچنین are adverbials (‘now’ and ‘likewise/in the same manner’) that frame the statement; من is the explicit subject. ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از meaning ‘from’, and درگاه شاه is the genitive phrase ‘the king’s court’ (درگاه = royal presence/court). The whole segment gives the origin/source of the forthcoming action: ‘now, from the king’s court I…’.
segments.s177-g312: This line strings together verbs that take سپاه (army) as their object. بنه, برنهم, برنشانم form a verbal sequence meaning roughly ‘I will array / I will set up / I will seat (place)’. In classical poetic style several verbs can be given in succession with one shared object; برنشانم literally ‘I will seat/place’ is idiomatic for setting troops in order. سپاه is the object ‘the army’. (Several of these verb forms are archaic/poetic.)
segments.s177-g153: اگر introduces a conditional subordinate clause ('if'). تو is the 2nd-person singular subject. بپیچی is the present/subjunctive form (ب‑ + present stem) of پیچیدن meaning 'turn (away)' or 'deviate' and here gives the protasis of the condition. ز governs the noun فرمان شاه ('from the king's command') and marks the source/thing being departed from; word order is poetic but the clause reads 'if you turn away from the king's command.'
segments.s177-g154: مرا با تو functions as a two-term expression 'between me and you' (مرا = me as object, با تو = with you/among you), while کین خیزد is the verb phrase 'vengeance/hatred arises' (خیزد = 'arises/comes into being'). The conjunction و links an additional noun رزمگاه ('battlefield') as the consequence; the clause literally means 'a grudge between you and me will arise, and (there will be) a battlefield.'
segments.s177-g155: فرستاده is the noun 'envoy/messenger'. گیوست is a poetic fusion of the name گیو with the copula (i.e. 'is Giv'); here it serves to identify the envoy. پیغام من ('my message') is a genitival phrase giving what the envoy carries; overall the clause states 'the envoy is Giv, (with) my message.'
segments.s177-g156: به marks direction/purpose ('to/for'). دستوری here functions as a deverbal/noun-like form meaning 'by (an) order' or 'under command' (i.e. 'by command'). نامدار modifies انجمن and means 'renowned'; انجمن is the destination 'assembly/council.' The phrase means 'to the renowned assembly by command.'
segments.s177-g157: ز پیش (literally 'from before') is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'in front of' or 'before' someone; پدر گیو means 'Giv's father' (possession expressed by juxtaposition). بنمود is the past tense 'showed' or 'displayed' and here takes پشت ('back') as its object: 'he showed/turned his back before Giv's father.'
segments.s177-g158: دلش is the nominal 'his heart' (دل + enclitic possessive ش). پر ز = 'full of' (پر 'full', followed by ز introducing the content). گفتارهای درشت is 'coarse/harsh words' (گفتارهای plural 'utterances' + adjective درشت 'coarse, rude'); together: 'his heart was full of harsh words.'
segments.s177-g159: بیامد is the past tense verb 'came'. به طوس is the prepositional phrase 'to Tus' (here Tûs is the addressee/place — the hero Tûs). سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general) and bگفت is the reporting verb 'and said' (ب‑ prefix + گفت). The sequence reports the arrival and speech: 'the spahbed came to Tûs and said...'.
segments.s177-g160: که introduces reported speech ('that'). این رای را is the object 'this counsel/plan' (رای with را as the definite/object marker). با تو = 'with you.' دیوست here functions as a proper name (a person, Diost/Dius) in apposition or as the pairing term, and جفت is predicate/adjective 'paired' or 'a match.' Literally: 'that this counsel pairs you with Diost.' Note poetic word order and the object marker را explicitly marking 'این رای.'
segments.s177-g289: Word order: the definite object 'دو فرزند ما را' (our two sons) comes before the adverb 'کنون' (now). The postposition 'بر' governs 'دو خیل' to mean 'over/above two hosts' (i.e. in command of two armies); 'را' is the Persian definite/object marker attached to the NP.
segments.s177-g290: 'بباید شدن' is an older/poetic periphrastic expression of obligation: 'بباید' = 'must/ought' plus the infinitive/auxiliary 'شدن' yields 'must be made / must become' (passive/causative sense). 'تا در اردبیل' uses 'تا' in the sense 'as far as / up to' followed by the locative 'در اردبیل' (in/at Ardabil).
segments.s177-g291: 'به مرزی' places location ('at/to a frontier'); the relative particle 'که' introduces a relative clause 'that/where'. 'آنجا دژ بهمنست' = 'there the fortress is Bahman' — note the copular/adjectival suffix '-ست' attached to 'بهمن' (poetic form meaning 'is Bahman' / 'called Bahman').
segments.s177-g292: 'همه‌ساله' is an adverb meaning 'yearly/annually' that modifies the following nominal phrase. 'پرخاش آهرمنست' is a nominal clause: 'پرخاش' (an attack/aggression) with the genitive/agent 'آهرمن' (Ahriman) plus the copula '-ست' = 'is Ahriman's attack' (i.e. Ahriman wages an annual assault).
segments.s177-g293: Syntactically this is a short clause in which 'ز آهرمن' ('from/of Ahriman') gives the source/cause and 'آتش‌پرست' ('fire‑worshipper') is in apposition or predicative relation. The first word is a poetic/archaic predicate form (here functioning to state the resulting condition); overall the segment expresses that the condition is caused by Ahriman.
segments.s177-g294: 'نباشد' is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'there is not / there will not be'. 'بران مرز' = 'on that frontier' ('بران' = 'on/thereon' + 'مرز' border). 'کس را نشست' literally 'a seat/setting for anyone' — Persian uses 'کس را' (anyone as affected person) plus the noun 'نشست' (sitting/place/seat) to say 'no one has a place there' or 'there is no seat for anyone.'
segments.s177-g295: 'ازیشان یکی' = 'one of them' (literally 'from them one'). The particle/word 'کان' here is archaic/poetic and works with the verb 'بگیرد' to express a hypothetical/contingent action ('should seize / take'). 'بگیرد' is present/subjunctive of 'گرفتن' meaning 'seize/take'; 'به تیغ' means 'by the sword' (instrumental: 'with the sword').
segments.s177-g296: 'ندارم' is the negated present of 'داشتن' used here idiomatically with 'دریغ' to mean 'I do not have reluctance / I will not begrudge'. 'ازو' = 'from him/of him' indicates the beneficiary, and 'تخت شاهی' = 'the royal throne/kingship' is the thing not withheld. Word order places the object ('تخت شاهی دریغ') after the verb phrase, typical in Persian.
segments.s177-g329: بشد is an archaic/epic past/preterite usage of شدن meaning “it happened / he set out”; the verb precedes the subject here (common in poetry). طوس is the subject (the hero Tūs). با + لشکری marks accompaniment/means (“with an army”) and جنگجوی is an attributive element after لشکری (adjective/noun used adjectivally: “a warlike army” or “army of fighters”).
segments.s177-g330: به تندی is an adverbial phrase (“with haste / swiftly”: به + noun making an adverb). سوی دژ is a prepositional direction phrase (“toward the fortress”). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن (“they set/placed”); here it appears in the idiom نهادند روی meaning “they set (their) face” = “they turned/steered themselves.” روی is the direct object of نهادند (literally “face”).
segments.s177-g331: سر باره here places the scene on/at the top; poetically سر (top) + باره (occasion/instance) yields a locative sense “on the summit / atop.” دژ is the fortress; بد is an adjective “bad / ill” qualifying the situation; اندر هوا means “in the air / out in the open” (اندر = in/within, هوا here = the physical air or open atmosphere). The whole phrase gives location and the unfavorable condition.
segments.s177-g332: ندیدند is the negated past tense “they did not see.” جنگ هوا is a compound meaning “battle/war in the air / fighting in the open” (جنگ = battle; هوا = air/open atmosphere). کس روا is a short predicative phrase: کس = anyone/someone and روا = fitting/permissible, together meaning “fit for no one” (i.e. no one was suitable for such fighting).
segments.s177-g333: سنانها is the plural subject “the spears.” ز introduces cause/agency “from / because of,” here followed by گرمی “heat.” همی is the archaic continuous/iterative particle (classical Persian) that precedes the verb and gives an ongoing or iterative sense. برفروخت is a poetic past meaning “kindled / flared up.” Thus: “the spears, because of the heat, kept blazing.”
segments.s177-g334: میان = “into / in the middle of.” زره = “armor.” مرد جنگی is a noun phrase where مرد (man/hero) is modified by جنگی (warlike/fighter) = “the warrior.” بسوخت is a past (intransitive or passive reading) meaning “was burned / was scorched.” Together: “they burned into the middle of the warrior’s armor.”
segments.s177-g335: جهان سر به سر is an idiom meaning “the whole world / entirely” (سر به سر = wholly). گفتی here functions as a substantive/statement (in epic usage it indexes what was said or the general verdict). از آتش است is a simple copular clause “is of/from fire” (از = of/from; آتش = fire; است = is). The clause reports the world's judgment that the air (or the situation) is fiery.
segments.s177-g336: هوا here shifts to the moral/psychological sense “desire / passion” (a common classical usage). دام = “trap,” آهرمن = Ahriman (the devil figure), and سرکش is an adjective “rebellious / insolent” modifying Ahriman; است is the copula. The clause says that desire is the trap set by rebellious Ahriman.
segments.s177-g321: راگر is the conditional 'if'; the clause reads literally 'Fariborz — if thus is (his) رای'. Est (است) is the copula 'is' linking رای (counsel/plan) to the predicate چنیـن ('thus'). Word order is somewhat inverted, typical of poetic Persian: the name comes first, then the conditional particle and the predicate about his رأی.
segments.s177-g322: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; لشکر بیارای is an imperative instruction 'array/prepare the army' (بیارای here is a hortative/imperative verb). و links the two imperatives. منشین is a negative imperative 'do not sit' (archaic form of 'ننشین'), ز is the preposition 'from/of' and پای 'foot'—z + pāy forms the idiom 'do not sit (from your feet)' meaning 'do not sit down/stand idle'.
segments.s177-g323: بشد here is a past/optative-type verb meaning 'rose/it happened that' (classical narrative past). Word order is inverted (verb before subject): 'بشد طوس' = 'Tūs rose'. با marks accompaniment/instrument 'with', کاویانی qualifies درفش as 'the Kayanian banner.'
segments.s177-g324: به پا + کردن is a light-verb construction 'to put on (shoes)'; here the participle کرده 'having done/put on' modifies the subject. اندرون 'inside/within' appears as part of the verbal phrase ('had put on inside' = 'had put on'), and زرینه کفش is the object 'golden shoes' (adjective after noun, poetic order). Overall: 'having put on golden shoes.'
segments.s177-g325: فریبرز کاووس is an appositive/genitive pairing: 'Fariborz (of) Kavus' — i.e. Fariborz, son/liegeman of Kavus. در introduces the locative phrase قلبگاه 'in the heart/center' (of the fortress/place).
segments.s177-g326: به پیش functions as 'before/ahead of' or 'in front of' (پیش = 'in the presence of / before'); اندرون is 'inside/within' and links to the following nouns: طوس و پیل و سپاه — 'Tūs and the elephant(s) and the army.' The phrase says he entered into the inner part ahead of those forces.
segments.s177-g327: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when.' نزدیک is 'near'; بهمن دژ is a named fortress ('Bahkan/Bahman' + دژ 'fortress') and functions as the locative object; اندر is the old/prepositional 'in/within', and رسید is the past 'reached/came.' Together: 'when (they) reached near the Bahman fortress.'
segments.s177-g328: همچو is a simile 'like/as'; همی is the classical continuous/progressive particle (marks ongoing action), and بردمید is the main verb here meaning something like 'rose up/burst forth/blazed' in poetic usage. The clause reads 'the earth, like fire, was (or began) to blaze.'
segments.s177-g313: پس is a discourse adverb 'then/so'. اندر is an older preposition 'in/for/at' that here introduces the beneficiary/location ('for Fereyberz' or 'at Fereyberz's command'). The coordinated nouns فریبرز و کوس و درفش list the addressee (a proper name) and the instruments (war-drum and standard) in the usual Persian noun-and-and sequence.
segments.s177-g314: هوا کرده is a light-verb construction: هوا (literally 'air') + کرده ('made/caused') = 'sent up/raised into the air' (i.e. set them aloft). از introduces the source/locative phrase 'from/at the hoofs of the horses' (سم اسپان), and بنفش is an adjective placed after the phrase that qualifies the standard as 'purple' (in classical poetry the color may be placed after the noun phrase).
segments.s177-g315: چو here is the conditional/temporal 'when/if'. فرزند را places 'the son/descendant' as a definite/topic marked noun (را), and the following nominal phrase فر و برز کیان ('the farr and the high/exalted rank of the Kians') is the predicate attributing royal glory and exalted station to that son — Persian often delays the copula until the next clause.
segments.s177-g316: بباشد is the subjunctive/capability copula 'be' (here part of the protasis 'if there be/if he has'). نبیره 'heir/scion' names the person not to be interposed; نبندد is the negative present/jussive form (ن + بندد) meaning 'do not bind/place', and میان 'between' gives the relational sense. Together: 'if [he] has such rank, do not place an heir between (them)'.
segments.s177-g317: بدو گفت شاه is a classical inverted order meaning 'the king said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said', شاه = 'the king'). ار is the conditional 'if'. تو is the second-person pronoun 'you'. رانی here functions as a verb meaning 'you flee/retreat/run away (from the front)' in context (its noun senses listed do not match this verbal use), and ز پیش = 'from the front / before'.
segments.s177-g318: زمانه is 'the time/age/fortune'. نگردد is the negative jussive/present of گردیدن 'to turn/change' meaning 'will not change/turn'. ز is the ablative 'from', آیین 'custom/established practice', and خوی + ش 'its own' ('خوی' = custom/habit and ش = possessive 'its'). The clause says 'time will not depart from its custom'.
segments.s177-g319: برای introduces reason/agent sense here ('for/by' or 'as to'), and خداوند خورشید و ماه is a titular/genitive-like phrase 'the Lord of the sun and moon' (classical epithet for the sovereign/Divine). The sequence names the agent/patron of what follows.
segments.s177-g320: توان is the modal 'can/is able', followed by ساخت (verbal 'to make/create' here) giving the potential 'can create/bring about'. پیروزی و دستگاه are the coordinated direct objects 'victory and order/establishment' (دستگاه here = system/establishment, i.e., a settled order).
segments.s177-g169: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle meaning 'still/continually' placed before the clause. بی من is a prepositional phrase 'without me' (بی + object). آیین و رای are coordinated objects ('custom/practice and counsel/plan') placed before the verb in typical Persian order. آورید is a 2nd-person plural verb form used as an exhortative/imperative or present-subjunctive 'bring/introduce' (verb-final).
segments.s177-g170: جهان را is the definite object marked with را. به نو کدخدای is a prepositional phrase indicating the result or appointment 'to (become) a new kâdkhodâ'; نو 'new' modifies کدخدای. آورید repeats the same 2nd‑person plural verb ('make/bring about/appoint'), here governing the whole result phrase.
segments.s177-g171: نباشم is a first‑person negative copula/subjunctive ('I will not be' / 'I am not'), here denying participation. بدین is a compound prepositional phrase 'in/with this' (ب + دین in the text; treat as the demonstrative prepositional phrase), and کار 'affair/matter' is its noun. همداستان is a predicate/adjective meaning 'accomplice / like‑minded' (i.e. 'I will not be complicit in this matter').
segments.s177-g172: ز here is the poetic genitive/partitive 'concerning / of', introducing the topic 'خسرو' (Khosrow, used here as sovereign). مزن is an imperative with negation implied in context ('do not relate/say' — literally 'do not strike' used idiomatically for 'do not tell'). پیش من means 'before me / in my presence', and داستان is 'story/tale' (object). The whole clause is a command: 'do not tell me the story of Khosrow.'
segments.s177-g173: جهاندار is a noun 'sovereign/ruler'. کز is the relative/complementizer 'who/that/from which', introducing the origin clause. تخم 'seed' + افراسیاب names the lineage ('from the seed of Afrasiab'). Together they form 'a sovereign who comes from Afrasiab's seed.'
segments.s177-g174: نشانیم is first‑person plural ('we will show/establish/seat') and governs the following clause as object/complement. بخت اندر آید به خواب is a subordinate clause: بخت 'fortune/fate' + اندر آید 'may come/come into' + به خواب 'in a dream' — an idiom meaning that an omen/fate appears in a dream. Grammatically, نشانیم introduces this as what we will demonstrate or produce.
segments.s177-g175: نخواهیم is the 1st‑person plural negative 'we do not want/wish'. شاه is the direct object 'a king'. از نژاد پشنگ is a genitive phrase 'from the lineage/stock of Pashang' (از + نژاد 'lineage' + the name پشنگ). Word order places the verb before its object for emphasis but the meaning is standard negative desire.
segments.s177-g176: فسیله (فَسِیله) is an archaic/poetic noun 'interval/respite, truce'. نه before نیکو negates it ('not good'). بود is the copula 'was/would be' (here a judgement about quality). با پلنگ 'with the panther/leopard' is an instrumental phrase. The clause states that a respite/truce with the panther would not be good (i.e. would be unwise).
segments.s177-g105: This clause states a past ongoing condition. کنون “now” sets the temporal frame. گیو is the subject; چندی (‘for a while’) is a temporal adverb modifying the following verb phrase. به سختی is a prepositional phrase expressing the state 'in/with hardship' and follows the temporal adverb before the verb. ببود is an archaic past-tense form of بودن used in epic/classical narrative to indicate a continuing past state (“was”/“remained”).
segments.s177-g106: This clause continues about the same subject (Giv). به توران gives the place/direction ('to/in Turan'); مرا is the direct object 'me' (here split in the token list into م + را). The verb جست (past of جستن) means 'sought/searched for' here. The conjunction و links the next coordinated predicate رنج آزمود, an idiomatic compound where آزمود (past of آزمایش‌کردن) takes رنج as its object and yields 'experienced/underwent suffering'. The subject (Giv) is omitted but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s177-g107: This is the protasis (conditional clause) introduced by اگر 'if'. نیز here functions as an emphatic/inclusive particle ('even/also'). رنجی uses the indefinite -ی to mean 'any suffering/any hardship'. نبودی is the classical negative past/subjunctive form of بودن used in counterfactual or hypothetical conditions ('had not been' / 'there was not'). جزین is a fused expression (جز + این) meaning 'except this / besides this' and closes the restriction on رنجی.
segments.s177-g108: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. با من 'with me' indicates accompaniment — the suffering that accompanied the speaker. بیامد is the past of آمدن 'came' describing how that hardship arrived/appeared. ز توران زمین is a locative/source phrase 'from the land of Turan' (ز is the poetic form of از, and زمین here means 'land/country'). The whole relative clause modifies 'this' (جزین) from the previous segment.
segments.s177-g109: This noun phrase begins with the epithet سرافراز 'proud/triumphant' placed before the noun it qualifies (a common poetic order). دو پهلوان 'two champions/heroes' is the head noun with its numeral. با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with an army,' indicating accompaniment — in classical Persian this follows the noun it modifies.
segments.s177-g110: پس here functions as 'after/behind' (and can also be temporal 'then'); ما is the oblique pronoun 'us' and together پس ما gives 'after us' (they came behind us). بیامد is the past of آمدن 'came'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as'; آتش 'fire' is the image of suddenness/ferocity; به راه 'on the road' is a locative phrase. Word order places the oblique pronoun before the verb and the simile phrase after it for poetic effect.
segments.s177-g111: من آن دیدم 'I saw that' is a reportive main clause (آن = 'that' refers forward to the content clause). از گیو here introduces the source/subject of the reported fact ('about Giv' / 'that Giv...'). کز is a fused relative particle (که + ز) meaning 'who/which/from which' and begins the subordinate description: کز پیل مست 'who/from a drunken elephant' — پیل 'elephant' modified by مست 'drunk', a compact poetic image.
segments.s177-g112: نبیند is the negative form of دیدن used in classical narrative; here it functions as a past/counterfactual sense 'did not / would not see'. به هندوستان gives the location 'in/to Hindustan' and is followed by بت‌پرست 'idolater' as the person not seen. The clause thus says that, owing to the drunken elephant or from its back, an idol-worshipper in Hindustan was not seen — the object (بت‌پرست) follows the locative in this poetic word order.
